amphitheater:acholla.desc: Punic-founded coastal city (mod. Ras Botria, Tunisia) that backed Julius Caesar in the 46 BCE African War and withstood a Pompeian siege; its extensive ruins include a Roman amphitheater and baths.

amphitheater:allifae.desc: Amphitheater of Roman Allifae (mod. Alife), built roughly 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE with a c. 70×45 m arena on a 110 m axis; buried until aerial photos revealed its outline in 1976, excavated 2007-09.

amphitheater:antioch.desc: An amphitheater is attested at Antioch on the Orontes, among the few built in the Hellenistic-Roman East and possibly dating to Julius Caesar's patronage, but its physical remains have never been securely located.

amphitheater:aquae-neri.desc: Amphitheater of the Gallo-Roman spa town Aquae Neri (Neriomagus), built late 1st–early 2nd century with a cavea some 86–96 m across; the settlement was abandoned by the later 3rd century.

amphitheater:bekalta.desc: Amphitheater of Thapsus, the Carthaginian and Roman port near Bekalta where Caesar defeated the Pompeians and Juba I at the decisive Battle of Thapsus (46 BCE); it measured about 240×150 ft, seating up to 16,000.

amphitheater:besan-on.desc: The Arenas of Besançon, a Roman amphitheater built in the later 1st century in Vesontio, roughly 122-130 m long, blending the 'classical' design of Arles/Nîmes with the earthwork type of Amiens and Tours; abandoned by the 4th century.

amphitheater:conimbriga.desc: Amphitheater of Conímbriga dating to the end of the Julio-Claudian dynasty; identified in 1993 and excavated from 2012-13, it held roughly 5,000 spectators before being partly demolished to supply stone for the city's late Roman defensive wall.

amphitheater:contributa.desc: A c. 72×65 m wood-and-earth amphitheatre built outside the walls of Contributa Iulia Ugultunia, a Baetican municipium; identified by geophysical survey in 2007 and confirmed by test excavation from 2008, dating to the 1st century AD.

amphitheater:djerba.desc: The amphitheatre of Roman Meninx, Djerba's chief town famed for its murex purple-dye trade, stood roughly 400 m from the city center; unexcavated, only its perimeter wall survives, likely from the Flavian-era monumentalization of the coast.

amphitheater:espejo.desc: A structure once reported as an amphitheatre on the hillside of Roman Ucubi (modern Espejo) has since been reidentified by archaeological study as a large cistern of the town's aqueduct; no amphitheatre is actually confirmed here.

amphitheater:falerone.desc: Built in the 1st century AD in Falerio Picenus, this elliptical amphitheatre (c. 120x105 m) seated an estimated 6,000 spectators behind a surrounding portico; today only fragments of its perimeter wall survive above ground.

amphitheater:formia.desc: The amphitheatre of Roman Formiae, precisely located near the town's railway station only in 2011, dates to the 1st century AD and stood alongside the city's theatre, huge cistern, and Temple of Janus.

amphitheater:genzano.desc: Within the imperial 'Villa of the Antonines,' birthplace of Antoninus Pius, ancient sources say Commodus killed wild beasts in an amphitheatre here, earning him the epithet 'Roman Hercules'; excavation in 2012-13 confirmed the c. 56 m structure.

amphitheater:jebel-moraba.desc: Discovered from the air in 1934, this amphitheatre lay about 500 m from the forum of an unidentified Roman settlement near Carthage; only earthen mounds marking its seating tiers and entrances remain visible today.

amphitheater:limoges.desc: Built under the Antonine dynasty in the 2nd century AD at Augustoritum, this amphitheatre (137x116 m) held an estimated 20,000-25,000 spectators, among the largest in Roman Gaul; buried in 1718 landscaping, its remains were rediscovered in 1966.

amphitheater:mesarfelta.desc: An amphitheater at this Numidian fort town on the Fossatum Africae frontier was restored by co-emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus, per a surviving dedicatory inscription crediting the Sixth Cohort of Commagenians as builders.

amphitheater:metz.desc: Built c. 100 CE at Divodurum Mediomatricorum, this was reputedly the third-largest amphitheater in the Roman world after the Colosseum and Capua, seating up to 25,000; its foundations were rediscovered in 1902 during construction of Metz's railway station.

amphitheater:minturno.desc: The amphitheater of Minturnae, a Roman colony on the Via Appia founded 296 BC, was still well preserved in the 18th century before near-total demolition; only fragmentary remains survive today beside the site's better-kept theater and aqueduct.

amphitheater:nijmegen.desc: Built at the start of the 2nd century outside the legionary fortress of Ulpia Noviomagus Batavorum, this 94x82m amphitheater (arena 58x46m) held at least 10,000 spectators and stayed in use into the 3rd century; only its street-paving outline survives.

amphitheater:portus-oval-structure.desc: Excavated in 2009 by University of Southampton archaeologists in Portus's Imperial Palace complex, this oval brick-and-concrete building — long misread as a theater by 19th-century excavator Rodolfo Lanciani, who had found only half of it — was Pantheon-sized and may have hosted private games for the emperor.

amphitheater:seressi.desc: Seressi, a Roman municipium of Africa Proconsularis (c. 30 BC-640 AD), corresponds to modern Oum el Abouab, Tunisia, whose ruins include a forum, unidentified public buildings, a theater, and an amphitheater.

amphitheater:thibari.desc: At ancient Thibaris, founded 103 BC as one of four colonies for Marius's veterans after his victory over Jugurtha, the remains of a Roman amphitheater measuring 61.2 by 51.5 meters were discovered accidentally in 1937.

building:102024695.desc: Built under the Carian satrap Mausolus in the 4th century BC and enlarged in the 2nd century AD under Rome, this theater held 10,000-13,000 spectators; a nearby platform was once mistaken for a temple of Ares.

building:107080283.desc: A small Roman-era theater (likely an odeon for music and recitation) at Antipatris, the city Herod the Great rebuilt around 9 BCE; it may never have been finished or was heavily stripped of stone before excavation.

building:115782359.desc: Palaeochristian basilica dedicated to Thessaloniki's patron saint, built in the early 5th century over the Roman bath where he was martyred; rebuilt in the 7th century after an earthquake and renowned for its Byzantine mosaics.

building:117499030.desc: Sixth-century Armenian monastery church near Yanal (ancient Soradir), Van province; an inscription records its dome and west entrance rebuilt in 582 under patron Saro Mahtesi. Its design reportedly inspired the Holy Cross church on Akhtamar.
building:117499030.wiki: Church of the Holy Cross at Soradir

building:118292710.desc: Large basilica (40x28 m) at Cuicul founded by bishop Cresconius around 400-411 AD, part of the city's Christian quarter with crypts and a monumental baptistery; Cresconius represented Cuicul as a Catholic bishop at the 411 Council of Carthage.

building:118337620.desc: A c. 150 m long Hellenistic commercial stoa excavated at Epano Skala in Mytilene, next to the moles of the city's ancient North Harbour, its principal commercial port.

building:118575.desc: A circular amphitheater in the vicus of Kastell Arnsburg, a Wetterau Limes cohort fort built around AD 90 under Domitian and garrisoned into the 3rd century; the amphitheater was identified by geophysical survey rather than excavation.

building:123971451.desc: Paleochristian basilica (5th-7th century) near Manacor, Mallorca, showing Syrian-Palestinian architectural influence, with a baptistery holding two fonts and an associated necropolis; excavated from 1912 under the initiative of Monsignor Aguiló.

building:135962387.desc: Extramural cemetery basilica at Salona where the martyred bishop Domnius was buried after his execution in 304 AD; a three-nave basilica was raised over his tomb in the 5th century after earlier destruction and later rebuilt again.

building:138007628.desc: Domed-hall church at Ani completed in 1215, funded by Armenian merchant Tigran Honents; its interior preserves an extensive fresco cycle on Saint Gregory the Illuminator, likely painted by Georgian artists under Georgian ecclesiastical control.

building:138349926.desc: A basilica church at Gerasa (Jerash) built in AD 611–614 under Bishop Genesius, reusing an earlier Roman temple; its nave and aisles were paved with dedicatory mosaics naming the founder, one of the last churches built before the Persian invasion.

building:138389.desc: Iciacum is identified with Yssac-la-Tourette; Gregory of Tours records an oratory here, the "domus Isiacensis," holding the relics of Saint Saturninus, which later passed to the Saint-Amable Abbey of Riom.

building:138424.desc: Locoteiacus is the ancient name of Ligugé, where Martin of Tours founded a monastic community around 361 on a former Gallo-Roman villa; excavation shows the earliest church on the site dates only to the 6th century.

building:138439.desc: Manatum is identified with Menat, site of a monastery founded around 480 and restored in the late 7th century by Saint Ménélée; later joined to Cluny, its abbey church became Menat's parish church in 1802.

building:138444.desc: Marciacum is identified with Marsat, whose church is mentioned by Gregory of Tours in the 6th century; its priory, later dependent on the abbeys of Mozac and Cluny, became a pilgrimage site for its Black Virgin.

building:138498.desc: Novivicum is identified with Neuvy-le-Roi, cited by Gregory of Tours as the "Novivicensis ecclesia"; from the second quarter of the 6th century its church held relics of Saint Vincent of Saragossa, a deacon martyred in 304.

building:138502.desc: Onia is identified with Heugnes, where Saint Ursus of Cahors founded a monastery around 491, one of three he established in Berry; it later became the Augustinian Abbey of Miseray, founded in 1142.

building:138503.desc: Identified with modern Orbigny (Indre-et-Loire); Gregory of Tours records that its 6th-century church, dedicated under Bishop Euphronius, held wonder-working relics of St Vincent of Saragossa brought via King Childebert I.

building:138519.desc: A sanctuary named Pompeiacum or Vernemetis in antiquity (Antonine Itinerary, Venantius Fortunatus); a basilica was raised in the early 5th century over the newly discovered tomb of St Vincent, whose relics were later moved to escape Viking raids. Its precise modern location (Caumont-sur-Garonne vs Le Mas-d'Agenais) is debated by scholars.

building:138530.desc: A monastery dedicated to St Quiricus, mentioned by Gregory of Tours, localized near modern St-Cirgues-sur-Couze in the Puy-de-Dôme (Barrington Atlas / Pleiades gazetteer).

building:138554.desc: A Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Saint-Georges-du-Bois, built in timber around AD 50, rebuilt in stone, then destroyed by fire within a century; at 62x53m it is the smallest of some forty known Gallic amphitheaters, holding roughly 1,500-2,000 spectators.

building:139971323.desc: Excavated in 1829-1830 at the Roman colony of Falerii Novi, this rock-cut theater (compared to the amphitheater at Sutri) yielded Augustan-era statues of Livia and her grandsons Gaius and Lucius Caesar; little survives above ground today.

building:142298158.desc: A mid-4th-century basilica church at the center of a planned Late Roman/early Byzantine mud-brick settlement in Egypt's Dakhla Oasis, revealed in 2026 excavations as the settlement's religious and communal focus, alongside a possible earlier house-church.

building:148180.desc: Excavated in the 1980s a few hundred meters from the Loupian Roman villa, this early-5th-century church ran 35m long and had an associated hexagonal baptismal font; it lies immediately north of the present 14th-century Ste-Cécile church.

building:148182.desc: A 6th-century paleochristian funerary complex excavated in 1991-92: a rectangular hall with sarcophagi beside an apsidal chapel, incorporating three reused 4th-century Christian funerary inscriptions; later refounded as a priory around 1000, dependent on Saint-Gilles abbey.

building:150310592.desc: Discovered in 1985 at the Gallo-Roman vicus of Ricciacum, this 2nd-century AD theater seated about 3,500 and is notable for rare surviving 'honor seats' with backrests and armrests, unique among known theaters in Gaul.

building:150531239.desc: A 2nd-century BC theater roughly 165 feet in diameter at the Lycian city of Cyaneae (Kyaneai), considered the best-preserved structure at the site, which also has an extensive rock-cut necropolis.

building:151530992.desc: A late-3rd/2nd-century BC theater at the foot of the acropolis of Iuvanum, a Samnite-then-Roman town in Abruzzo; only the lowest seven rows of seating and part of the orchestra are still visible.

building:154948553.desc: The rock-cut cavea of a theater, roughly 115 meters across, was identified in 1997 on the Zegreg Tepe hillside at Tavium, capital of the Galatian Trocmi tribe and a Roman-era crossroads town.

building:156802663.desc: A 13th–14th-century Caetani-family fortress built around the Augustan-era mausoleum of Caecilia Metella (c. 30–10 BC) on the Via Appia, using the tomb as its keep to control the road and its tolls.

building:157797874.desc: Begun in 1160 on a hill where a 4th-century cathedral had replaced a Roman-era sanctuary; John Calvin preached here from 1536, making it the centre of the Genevan Reformation.

building:160004728.desc: One of two 6th-century Byzantine churches at Theodorias (ancient Olbia), refounded by Justinian in 539; the cruciform West Church preserves an in-situ mosaic of two deer flanking a cross, evoking Psalm 42.

building:160818.desc: Benedictine monastery founded c. 500 CE on the islet where St. Martin of Tours reportedly lived as a hermit; it grew into a wealthy abbey with estates across Liguria, France and Spain by the 12th century.

building:163716294.desc: An Achaemenid satrapal palace on a fortified 450×425 m garden estate near Karačamirli, Azerbaijan, occupied 5th–4th century BC; German-Azerbaijani excavations since 2006 found mudbrick walls up to 1.75 m tall and a bell-shaped stone column base.

building:167755.desc: Traditionally identified as the site of the Council of Epaone (517 CE), where 25 Burgundian bishops under Avitus of Vienne issued 40 canons; possibly linked to a Merovingian basilica excavated near Saint-Romain-d'Albon.

building:170580013.desc: Late Byzantine cross-in-square church built around 1300, probably the katholikon of the Monastery of the Almighty; its c. 1315 frescoes survive under plaster from its 1520s conversion into the Yakup Pasha mosque.

building:177101519.desc: The Roman amphitheater of Vergi at Berja (Almeria), a semi-excavated elliptical arena measuring 43x26.6m, in use during the 1st-2nd centuries AD and one of only seven known Roman amphitheaters in Andalusia.

building:177668735.desc: Small deme theater at Ikarion, cult center of the Rural Dionysia and reputed home of Thespis, traditionally credited with inventing tragedy; excavated in 1888-89 by the American School, revealing stone proedria seats beside the orchestra.

building:184642281.desc: A roughly 2,000-seat theater built for the Pausilypon villa of the Roman knight Vedius Pollio near Naples; on his death in 15 BC the estate passed to Augustus and remained an imperial retreat for his successors.

building:187306.desc: An 11th-century circular church, excavated in 1995–97, that held the stone tomb of Benno of Molzbichl (ministerial of the Bishop of Brixen); rebuilt in late-Gothic style in the 15th century, with 1739 Baroque frescoes by Balthasar Klenkh.

building:187449.desc: Excavations at Laubendorf uncovered a 5th-century hall church (13.7×6.8 m) with round apse, priest's bench, and cathedra; a liturgical chalice found here is considered Austria's oldest surviving communion vessel.

building:187507.desc: An early Christian church stood on the Pfarrbichl north of Lienz by the 5th century, on the site later rebuilt from 1430 as the Gothic three-aisled parish church of St. Andrä.

building:187534.desc: Excavations (1978–1982) beneath the later Benedictine nunnery of Säben uncovered three Early Christian churches, a baptistery, and a burial ground, confirming the site as a bishop's seat from the 5th to 8th centuries.

building:188321148.desc: Pompeii's civic forum, laid out as a pedestrian-only piazza in the 2nd century BC and flanked by the Capitolium (Temple of Jupiter), basilica, comitium, macellum, and Eumachia building; sealed and preserved by the AD 79 eruption.

building:191480805.desc: A Christian monastery and bishop's palace on Siniyah Island, dated to 534–656 CE, with a church, refectory, kitchen, cistern, and baptismal font; only the second monastery yet found in the UAE.

building:199686145.desc: Roman theater of the imperial-era colony of Privernum (modern Priverno), part of a marble-decorated public complex with statues and portraits of the imperial family; known mainly from excavation and epigraphic finds rather than literary sources.

building:204612618.desc: Roman-period theater at Tios (Tieion) on the Black Sea coast, judged in the late 19th century among the best-preserved in Asia Minor with an estimated capacity of about 7,000; quarried for building stone before excavations began in 2006.

building:20481513.desc: The amphitheater of Uchi Maius (Henchir ed-Douamis, Tunisia), long misidentified as a theater until Italian excavations confirmed its true form; the city was raised to a Roman colony under Severus Alexander in AD 230.

building:210536349.desc: A mid-4th-century basilica church (Building 7) at Amheida, ancient Trimithis, in Egypt's Dakhla Oasis; a 2013 excavation found sealed crypts beneath its sanctuary holding Egypt's oldest known Christian burials.

building:211761080.desc: Forum of Bagacum Nerviorum, capital of the Nervii, built in two phases across the 1st-2nd centuries AD; at c. 2.5 ha it is the largest Roman forum yet found in France, with a surviving cryptoporticus excavated 1942-1976.

building:216926.desc: The Red Church near Perushtitsa, a rare solid-brick basilica of the late 5th–early 6th century, began as a 4th–5th-century martyrium and was later expanded with mosaic floors beneath a dome once over 32 m high.

building:217728890.desc: A small apsidal Early Christian basilica outside the walls of ancient Akragas, sited in the valley below the so-called Temple of Juno on the city's eastern slopes.

building:221152517.desc: Small Roman theater at Adraha (modern Daraa, Syria), part of a classical quarter south of the Ottoman-era town alongside a colonnaded decumanus and a temple; known chiefly from surface ruins rather than formal excavation.

building:223617668.desc: New royal residence built at Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC) in the Kasr district; its largely undisturbed remains were uncovered in Robert Koldewey's German excavations (1899–1917) alongside the Ishtar Gate and Processional Way.

building:224949836.desc: One of two interlinked rock-hewn church complexes at Lalibela; its churches, including Bet Gabriel-Rafael (reached by a rock bridge called the 'Way to Heaven'), Bet Merkorios, Bet Amanuel, and Bet Abba Libanos, symbolize the heavenly Jerusalem.

building:233803923.desc: The amphitheater of Thermae Himeraeae (Termini Imerese, Sicily), a modest 98x75m structure with an unusual double ambulatory that once seated up to 4,000 - the third-largest amphitheater in Sicily - though poorly preserved today.

building:236087657.desc: A three-aisled 5th-century paleochristian basilica rediscovered in 1951-52 near Son Bou beach, Menorca, with an adjoining rock-cut necropolis; recent Sanisera Institute excavations suggest a later, 7th-8th century construction date.

building:236314821.desc: The Hellenistic agora of Magnesia on the Maeander, an enclosed square lined with Ionic stoas and shops; excavation of the stoa's lower galleries recovered painted frescoes documenting everyday commercial life.

building:236384.desc: The ecclesia Britonensis (Britonia), a diocese for Romano-British migrant settlers in Gallaecia recognized at the Council of Lugo in 569, with its first bishop Maeloc attested at the Second Council of Braga in 572; suppressed 716, later merged into Mondoñedo.

building:236512.desc: The legionary amphitheater of Legio VII Gemina's fortress at Leon, one of only about five known legionary amphitheaters in the Roman Empire; built in phases from the 1st century AD, it held roughly 5,000 spectators across fifteen tiers.

building:236531.desc: Magnetum (Meinedo), a diocese of the Suevic Kingdom of Galicia whose bishop Viator attended the Second Council of Braga in 572; suppressed in 585 and likely the precursor see later absorbed into the Diocese of Porto.

building:236593.desc: Pampliega, near Burgos, site of the Visigothic royal monastery of San Vicente where King Wamba retired after his forced abdication in 680 and died in 688; his remains were later moved to Toledo Cathedral.

building:236681.desc: The church of Santa María de Temes (Carballedo, Lugo) preserves Galicia's oldest Christian artifacts: a marble sarcophagus lid carved c.315-325 with biblical scenes, and a granite inscription reading FIDES SPES CARITAS, both found in 1974.

building:237118389.desc: A three-aisled early Christian basilica of the 6th century (roughly 50x19m) with mosaic floors, excavated beneath the later chapel of Agios Nikolaos at the harbor of Chersonissos/Limani Khersonesou, Crete.

building:237150019.desc: Egalgabarinutukua ("Palace That Has No Rival"), the palace Sargon II of Assyria built at his new capital Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad) c. 717–706 BC on an artificial platform straddling the city wall; at roughly 10 hectares it was the largest Assyrian palace ever built.

building:241704649.desc: Built over the ruins of a Roman temple to Diana Tifatina on Monte Tifata; rebuilt 1072–1087 under Abbot Desiderius of Montecassino and decorated with Byzantine-influenced frescoes uncovered in 1868.

building:246233.desc: A Visigothic-period chapel was built here over a late Roman villa that has yielded 4th–5th century pottery and a bronze Constantinian coin (330–346); a possible baptistery adjoined the nave.

building:246245.desc: Monastery founded in the 580s by the Visigothic chronicler John of Biclaro after returning from exile in Constantinople, governed by a rule he wrote himself; tentatively identified with Vallclara/Cabacés in Tarragona province.

building:246507.desc: A paleochristian basilica and adjoining necropolis of stone sarcophagi were exposed here during construction of an irrigation channel; four Corinthian-style capitals looted from the site now support the altar of the parish church of Gerb.

building:246534016.desc: Roman theater of Forum Iulii (Fréjus), rebuilt in stone under Tiberius on the site of an earlier wooden theater, with an original capacity of about 2,000; restored and returned to performance use in 1997.

building:246557.desc: The monasterium Pauliacense, recorded by Gregory of Tours in his Glory of the Martyrs, held a church of St Saturninus; identified with Saint-Sernin in the Aude, it had passed by 1085 to the abbey of La Grasse.

building:246616.desc: Site of the Roman fort Castrum Octavianum and, from the 5th century, a martyrium over the tomb of St Cucuphas; refounded as a Benedictine abbey in the 9th century, it became the wealthiest monastery in the county of Barcelona.

building:246621.desc: Excavations in 1984 uncovered a 6th–7th century apse with a horseshoe plan and an altar on small columns set in an opus signinum floor beneath the church, evidence of an early paleochristian basilica; the parish is first recorded in 972.

building:246623.desc: Monastery traditionally founded in the 6th century as San Martín de Asán, renamed for abbot Victorianus in the 11th century; a royal pantheon holding the tombs of Sobrarbe kings Gonzalo I and Íñigo Arista.

building:246699.desc: A semicircular smoke-signal tower flanking the church of the Benedictine monastery of Sant Feliu de Guíxols, built in the 10th century atop 5th-century remains; watchmen burned wet straw to warn of seaborne raiders.

building:248782117.desc: One of five known theaters at Cyrene, on the acropolis ridge; a minor, conventionally numbered structure distinct from the much larger Greek Theatre (Theatre 1) near the Sanctuary of Apollo, with little published individual documentation.

building:24962619.desc: Well-preserved 2nd-3rd century Roman theater at Thubursicum Numidarum (Khamissa, Algeria), considered one of the finest in North Africa, with a 56.8 m cavea and 43.6 m stage front; first excavated by French scholars, 1900-1922.

building:249909.desc: Gallo-Roman villa or roadside inn on the Bordeaux–Astorga road near Sorde-l'Abbaye; its baths were built in the 3rd century and remodeled in the 4th, with mosaic pavements from the later 4th century; listed a monument historique in 1997.

building:255953.desc: A station on the Antonine Itinerary's road from Gades, tied to the Temple of Hercules Gaditanus on the islet later called Sancti Petri, near modern San Fernando, Cádiz.

building:282907842.desc: One of several elite residences (labeled K, L, J, M) inside the walled citadel of Sargon II's capital Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad); presumed home of a high Assyrian official, but unlike neighboring Residence L (owned by Sargon's brother Sin-ahu-usur), no inscriptions identify its occupant.

building:285468794.desc: Roman theater at the coastal colony of Castrum Novum, built in the Imperial age facing the sea, with a ~30 m stage and seating for roughly 1,000; brick stamps of Marcus Aurelius indicate 2nd-century restructuring, confirmed in excavations resumed since 2010.

building:288803012.desc: Early Christian triple-aisled basilica at ancient Tegea, published by Anastasios Orlandos in 1953, noted for its 5th-century mosaic floor depicting the four rivers of Eden and the months of the year with a metrical inscription.

building:293222275.desc: Mud-brick Greek theater built for Babylon's Greek community, probably dating to Alexander the Great's reign and rebuilt in the 2nd century AD per a Greek repair inscription; excavated by Robert Koldewey, final diameter about 24.8 m.

building:294093254.desc: The last and grandest royal palace built on the Pergamon acropolis (2nd century BC), centered on a peristyle courtyard famed for Hellenistic mosaics signed by the artist Hephaistion, including a ring-necked parakeet mosaic now in Berlin's Pergamon Museum.

building:295344385.desc: The northern group of Lalibela's rock-hewn churches — Biete Medhane Alem, Biete Maryam, Biete Golgotha Mikael, Biete Meskel, and Biete Denagel — linked by tunnels and trenches; five-aisled Biete Medhane Alem is considered the largest monolithic church in the world.

building:295344387.desc: A circular assembly hall (ekklesiasterion) unique in the Greek world, dominating Metapontum's agora and seating up to 7,500-8,000; converted into a theater around 325-300 BC, later repaired, then sealed up as a fortress.

building:303354808.desc: An ornately decorated elevated suite in Adad-nirari III's palace at Kalhu (Nimrud), possibly the residence of his mother and regent Sammu-ramat (the legendary Semiramis); re-excavated in 2022–2023 by a Penn Museum/Iraqi State Board of Antiquities team, which found inscribed door-sill texts.

building:309437155.desc: Royal residence built at Kalhu (Nimrud) by the Assyrian king Adad-nirari III (r. 810–783 BC), whose mother Sammu-ramat, later mythologized as Semiramis, served as regent early in his reign and may have resided there.

building:31748292.desc: A 10th–13th-century Armenian monastic complex of four churches, a gavit, and outbuildings on Paitatap Mountain; the Surb Astvatsatsin church (1198) and main church (1205) are renowned for their intricate carved ornament.

building:326045393.desc: Main civic square of Aphrodisias, a colonnaded plaza c. 202 x 72 m enclosed by Ionic porticoes; its north colonnade was dedicated by C. Julius Zoilos in the late 1st century BC, the south portico added later under Tiberius.

building:329343086.desc: One of two fora at Mactaris (Roman Mactar, Tunisia); this earlier square lies beside the Arch of Trajan, the triumphal arch dedicated to Trajan in AD 116 that survives as the site's best-preserved monument.

building:335577249.desc: The Republican-era 'minor amphitheater' of Puteoli (Pozzuoli), used alongside the later Flavian Amphitheater; here in AD 66 Nero's freedman Patrobius staged lavish beast-hunts honoring the visiting Armenian king Tiridates I, per Cassius Dio.

building:339848370.desc: Roman-era odeon/small theater at ancient Kelenderis (Aydıncık), first traced from a surviving semicircular diazoma wall; excavations from 1989 and documentation since 2020 have exposed its cavea, orchestra, and stage.

building:353853857.desc: A subterranean Paleochristian basilica discovered in 2003 at the eighth mile of the Via Tiburtina, reusing an earlier stone quarry; dated to the late 4th–mid 5th century CE and possibly linked to the cult of the martyr Symphorosa.

building:354239692.desc: Three-aisled basilica built at Portus between the late 4th and early 5th century over the tomb of the martyr Hippolytus; destroyed in the 455 Vandal sack of Rome and rebuilt by Pope Leo III, with the saint's sarcophagus and Leo III's ciborium now in the adjacent Antiquarium.

building:354671742.desc: Large three-aisled basilica built at Salamis-Constantia in the late 5th–6th century CE with twin atria and marble furnishings imported from Proconnesus, likely dedicated to the Holy Cross; damaged in the Arab raid of 648/649 but remained in use by a small monastic community.

building:361097850.desc: Late 5th-century church in Thessaloniki, katholikon of the Latomos Monastery, famed for its apse mosaic of the Vision of Ezekiel ('Christ of Latomos'), said to have been hidden during Iconoclasm and rediscovered later; part of the UNESCO-listed Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki.

building:364392645.desc: Monumental Gallo-Roman theater built around 120 AD east of Samarobriva (Amiens), with a 132 m facade ranking among Gaul's largest and estimated capacity over 5,000; unexpectedly discovered in 2006 excavations.

building:368305524.desc: A circular orchestra in the Athenian Agora used for dramatic and musical contests before the Theatre of Dionysus existed; a replacement set of markers was made by sculptors Kritios and Nesiotes in 476 BC.

building:372021190.desc: Stone-built theater of Carthaea (Karthaia) on Kea, dated to the 4th century BC and seating nearly 1,000; first examined by Danish antiquarian Peter Oluf Brønsted in 1812, later excavated and restored 2011-2015.

building:38100986.desc: This amphitheater stood at Thizica, a minor Roman civitas in Africa Proconsularis; Augustine of Hippo records that its bishop Novellus was executed by Donatists around 313 AD.

building:410058374.desc: A Visigothic-era church (probably 7th century) built within the older Punic oppidum of Silla del Papa near Tarifa, possibly founded by Pimenius, Bishop of Asidonia (Medina Sidonia); excavated since 2014, it remained in domestic use into the early Emirate period.

building:415768871.desc: A circiform funerary basilica built by Pope Mark in AD 336 with Constantine's funding on the Via Ardeatina, part of the Callixtus catacomb complex; rediscovered in 1991 and excavated 1993–2013, revealing Pope Mark's tomb.

building:422867.desc: The Capuchin convent (church consecrated 1579, patronized by Gregory XIII) was built beside the remains of a 1st-century BC Roman villa traditionally identified as Cicero's Tusculanum, where he set his Tusculan Disputations.

building:423041.desc: The oratory of San Clemente, one of the twelve monasteries St Benedict founded in the Aniene valley near Subiaco in the early 6th century, was built by reusing the baths and nymphaeum of Nero's ruined villa (Sublaqueum).

building:423051.desc: A church on the Tiber opposite the Basilica of St Paul Outside the Walls, traditionally marking where relics of the Alexandrian saints Cyrus and John landed c. AD 400; the present building (9th century) incorporates an earlier Roman tomb, and its name is a corruption of "Abbas Cyrus."

building:424010351.desc: A stoa roughly 62 m long, probably built in the 4th century BC, within the Pan-Hellenic sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios on Mount Lykaion, part of an athletic complex with hippodrome, stadium and bathhouse used for the Lykaia games.

building:427981474.desc: Roman theater of Augusta Bagiennorum (Bene Vagienna), 1st century AD, with a cavea about 57 m across holding ~3,000 spectators; decorated with Luni marble and imported colored marbles, identified in the late 19th century and restored in the 1950s.

building:433082.desc: Founded by St Benedict of Nursia around AD 529 on a hilltop site previously home to a temple of Apollo, this was the first house of the Benedictine order and where Benedict composed his monastic Rule; sacked by the Lombards c. 570.

building:433084.desc: A rural monastic estate (the domusculta of Capracorum) 15km north of Rome between Veii and Formello, founded c.780 by Pope Hadrian I; rediscovered in the 1958 South Etruria Survey and excavated by Charles Daniels in 1962-64.

building:436809109.desc: Theater of the Roman colony Colonia Genetiva Iulia (Urso), founded by Julius Caesar for army veterans in 44 BC; its cavea rests against a natural hillside and has been documented by scholars since the 1870s.

building:437043462.desc: A church built perhaps in the 7th-8th century on the ruins of the Roman town of Peltuinum on the Via Claudia Nova, reusing two Roman columns with capitals and limestone blocks from the site; badly damaged in the 2009 L'Aquila earthquake.

building:438390658.desc: Civic forum of Roman Tergeste on Trieste's San Giusto hill, developed from the Augustan period; its later 1st-century-AD Propylaeum and basilica led to a Capitoline temple precinct, remains now incorporated into Trieste Cathedral's foundations.

building:439136375.desc: Built c. 878–864 BC by Ashurnasirpal II as Assyria's first grand royal residence, its courtyards lined with alabaster reliefs of the king, winged spirits and campaigns; rediscovered by Layard in the 1840s, with royal queens' tombs found beneath it in 1988.

building:448083500.desc: A ruined fifth-century basilica on the Karpass peninsula, an episcopal church founded by Bishop Philon on the site of ancient Carpasia's Roman harbour, with a triapsidal plan, synthronon and baptistery; sacked in Arab raids and abandoned after 802.

building:448119705.desc: Theater of the Roman municipium of Singilia Barba near Antequera, with a cavea about 52 m across; known since the 16th century, its upper seating tiers were later quarried to build Antequera's Church of San Juan de Dios.

building:458747378.desc: Theater of ancient Telmessos (modern Fethiye), begun in the late Hellenistic or Augustan period with its stage added around 200 AD; excavated 1992-95, it held about 6,000 spectators across 28 rows.

building:475264756.desc: A Cistercian abbey founded in 1190 by Cathal Crobhdearg Ua Conchobair, King of Connacht, and colonised by monks from Boyle Abbey; the king died a monk here in 1224, and its chancel preserves rare medieval wall paintings.

building:480714924.desc: A paleochristian sanctuary on a side branch of the Via Francigena's Cimina route, built over a 4th-century pagan cemetery associated with Flavio Eusebio; its vault bears 53 Roman uncial graffiti, the largest such corpus known.

building:480919150.desc: Begun by Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) in the citadel's southwest corner and left unfinished at his death, largely built from reused stone and inscribed reliefs stripped from the dismantled Central Palace of Tiglath-pileser III.

building:480919151.desc: Grand royal residence built by Tiglath-pileser III (r. 744–727 BC); later dismantled by Esarhaddon, who reused its inscribed stone reliefs and masonry to construct the nearby Southwest Palace, leaving little standing in situ.

building:48637573.desc: Roman theater at ancient Soli, Cyprus, dated by coin finds to the mid-1st century AD and used into the 4th century; excavated in 1927 by the Swedish Cyprus Expedition under Alfred Westholm and rebuilt in 1962-64, it seated about 3,500.

building:499970655.desc: A Π-shaped colonnaded agora (about 1 hectare) at the sanctuary of Poseidon at Onchestos in Boeotia, built around the late 4th century BC as a political and religious meeting place for the Boeotian League.

building:508370540.desc: A Roman villa rustica on the Mirna river estuary in Istria, later fortified in Late Antiquity with a tower and a sacral complex including the church of Santa Croce; under excavation since 2008.

building:511519022.desc: Theater at Noviodunum Diablintum (Jublains), built over an earlier structure probably in the later 1st century AD; unusually elliptical and lacking a stage wall, it was donated to the town by the decurion Orgetorix per a surviving inscription.

building:523291014.desc: Theater of ancient Rhodiapolis in Lycia, with 16 surviving rows of seating; it stood before the funerary monument of Opramoas, the city's wealthy 2nd-century AD benefactor, and was fully excavated after 2006.

building:544710174.desc: Theater of the mountaintop Pisidian city Termessos, built in the Hellenistic 2nd century BC and remodeled by the Romans in the 2nd century AD; its diazoma-divided cavea seated 4,000-5,000, with animal pens beneath the stage for beast shows.

building:544711870.desc: U-shaped stoa in the extramural sanctuary of Marasà Sud/Centocamere at Locri Epizephyrii, built in the 7th century BC and enlarged in the 6th; over 370 votive pits (bothroi) tied to the cult of Aphrodite were found within it.

building:55405152.desc: Theater of ancient Tralles (Aydin), birthplace of Anthemius, architect of Hagia Sophia; the ruins, now on private farmland, are noted for the storks nesting atop them.

building:558037429.desc: A rare Gallo-Roman hybrid theater-amphitheater whose cavea covers only half the elliptical ring; built around the 2nd century AD for roughly 5,000 spectators, excavated 1862–1901, and used into the 4th century.

building:570229761.desc: A large administrative complex on the Amathus acropolis, one of the earliest monumental stone buildings of Iron Age Cyprus, with its first phase dated to the late 9th century BC; still under excavation by the French School at Athens.

building:575107588.desc: Greek theatre built in the late 3rd century BC under Hiero II of Syracuse, with nine wedge-shaped seating sections and twelve rows holding roughly 700 spectators; still hosts performances today.

building:585690561.desc: Built under Augustus (late 1st century BC), Mediolanum's theatre had a 95m-wide cavea and 20m stage, holding an estimated 7,000-9,000 spectators; its remains lie beneath the Palazzo Turati.

building:586620869.desc: Excavated by Max Mallowan in 1949–50 (initially dubbed the "1949 building") just north of the Nabu temple Ezida; yielded roughly 230 cuneiform tablets recording the activities of Kalhu's provincial governors.

building:586635000.desc: Built by Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC) as the ekal māšarti ("review palace"/arsenal) in Kalhu's southeast corner, enclosing c. 30 ha; excavations by Mallowan and David Oates (1950s–60s) recovered thousands of the famous Nimrud ivories.

building:593911178.desc: This elliptical amphitheater (94×66 m) at Trebula Mutuesca was partly cut into the rocky hillside, had an underground gallery for stage machinery, and was restored under Trajan per surviving dedicatory inscriptions.

building:612060178.desc: A 2nd-century BC theatre, among the last built in Greek style in Calabria, with a freestanding stone cavea originally holding around 1,200 spectators; discovered in 1883, fully excavated by 1925.

building:62047881.desc: Forum of the Samnite town of Iuvanum (Montenerodomo, Abruzzo), which became a Roman municipium after the Social War; excavated since the 1960s, it comprised a basilica, temple and tabernae beside a rock-cut theatre.

building:622079673.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Phoinike in Epirus, built mid-3rd century BC and renovated in the 2nd, seating roughly 7,000 with a 30m scaenae frons; remained in use after Rome annexed Epirus under Augustus.

building:625262900.desc: Built by Tigranes the Great around 69 BC for Greek drama performed by artists gathered from across his empire — the first known theatre in Armenia; still unfinished when Lucullus took the city and celebrated his triumph there.

building:66838501.desc: Main forum of the Roman colony Augusta Raurica near Basel, founded c. 44 BC by Lucius Munatius Plancus; housed the temple of Jupiter, a basilica, and the town council's assembly chamber.

building:688857980.desc: Two-terraced Roman forum of Thessalonica with two-storey stoas and baths; its well-preserved Odeon hosted both musical performances and gladiatorial/beast fights, and the complex was uncovered accidentally in the 1960s during construction work.

building:69311541.desc: Roman theatre at Aquinum on the Via Latina in the Liri valley, birthplace of the poet Juvenal; excavations exposed 22 radial cavea walls in reticulate-faced concrete plus part of the stage and orchestra steps.

building:694530902.desc: A royal building with the longest construction sequence of any structure at Kalhu, standing near the Nabu temple; destroyed by fire when the city fell to the Babylonian-Median coalition in 612 BC, excavated by Max Mallowan.

building:694724623.desc: Chance find in 1929 in the gardens of Palazzo Romano; built in the Augustan period, its six-wedge cavea (75m diameter) held some 5,000-6,000 spectators for comedies and tragedies, with later Antonine-era statuary added.

building:697765430.desc: Carved into a hillside outside Cuicul's walls, this two-tier theater seated about 3,000; a surviving inscription naming patrons Caius Julius Crescens and Caius Julius Didius Crescentianus dates its dedication to AD 161.

building:698029509.desc: Forum of the Roman colony of Scolacium (founded 123-122 BC over the Greek Skylletion) in Calabria, paved uniquely in brick and flanked by the curia, Caesareum and Capitolium; re-monumentalized under emperor Nerva.

building:702050141.desc: Excavated in 1967 south of the Gosbecks Romano-Celtic temple near Camulodunum; an earthwork structure rebuilt in stone-revetted turf and clay, estimated to have seated up to 5,000 - the largest of Britain's five known Roman theaters.

building:702770983.desc: Theater of Roman Albintimilium, built roughly late 2nd-early 3rd century AD with its cavea partly cut into bedrock, holding about 2,000 spectators; systematically excavated from the 1930s onward.

building:713959974.desc: A Greek theater at Parium on the Hellespont, rebuilt by the Romans in the 2nd and early 3rd centuries AD; the site has been under renewed archaeological excavation since 2005.

building:716466643.desc: The oldest theater in Roman Africa, dedicated in AD 1-2 and partly financed by local benefactor Annobal Tapapius Rufus (who also built the city market); cut into a hillside once used as a cemetery, second in size in Africa only to Sabratha's theater.

building:717575054.desc: One of two theaters on the Mont Châtelet site near Breteuil (Oise); this smaller theater dates to the 1st century AD, while the adjacent grand theater (late 1st-early 2nd century) held about 4,000. Located via 1955 aerial photography and excavated 1956-1986.

building:727686421.desc: A well-preserved theater on the slopes of ancient Arykanda in Lycia, built around the mid-1st century BC with 20 rows of seats in seven wedges; its stage building was remodeled in the late 2nd-early 3rd century AD.

building:748147226.desc: The ekal māšarti (armory/arsenal) of Sargon II's capital Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad, built 717–706 BC), sited in the lower city; excavated by the Oriental Institute of Chicago team led by Gordon Loud in the 1930s.

building:748147227.desc: Residence of Sîn-aḫu-uṣur, brother of Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) and grand vizier of the Assyrian Empire, built within the citadel of Sargon's new capital Dūr-Šarrukīn (Khorsabad) in the late 8th century BC.

building:748147228.desc: One of several royal residences Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (Khorsabad); excavated by the Oriental Institute of Chicago in the 1930s (Loud & Altman).

building:756231346.desc: The Theatre of Balbus, Rome's third public theatre, was built in 13 BC by Lucius Cornelius Balbus the Younger from spoils of his African campaign; its rear portico, excavated from 1981, gives the site its modern name Crypta Balbi.

building:763220261.desc: Hellenistic theatre (2nd century BC, with Roman-era alterations) at Laodicea on the Lycus, cut directly into bedrock and seating roughly 8,000-10,000; damaged by earthquakes in the 3rd century and in 368 AD, it was excavated and restored 2003-2021.

building:764638886.desc: Built in squared-block (opus quadratum) masonry at the Punic-Roman port of Thapsus, this amphitheater stood at the city where Julius Caesar decisively defeated Metellus Scipio and King Juba I of Numidia in 46 BC.

building:786052705.desc: Theatre of Stratos, capital of the Acarnanian League, begun in the late 4th century BC as a stoa-like stage building; enlarged through three phases into the 2nd century BC, it held some 6,000-7,000 spectators and is the largest theatre known in Acarnania.

building:792804148.desc: The South Agora (Portico of Tiberius) at Aphrodisias, a 215x70m colonnaded piazza whose north stoa was dedicated by the aristocrat Diogenes to Tiberius; later held a 170m ornamental pool, excavated 2013-2017.

building:832363479.desc: Built at the sacred festival site of Dion probably under Philip V (221-179 BC), this Hellenistic theatre hosted the Macedonian kings' Olympia games; tradition holds that Euripides's Archelaus and Bacchae, written during his stay at the Macedonian court, were staged there.

building:836083242.desc: One of several royal residences Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (Khorsabad), excavated by the Oriental Institute of Chicago in the 1930s.

building:836258799.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Magarsus, the sea-facing sanctuary port of Mallos in Cilicia where Alexander the Great reportedly sacrificed to Athena Magarsis before Issus; excavated from 2013, it seated about 3,000 and is noted as the only ancient theatre with a sea view.

building:838441539.desc: Forum of Clunia Sulpicia, a conventus capital in Hispania Tarraconensis near Peñalba de Castro, Burgos; includes a rectangular basilica, tribunal and tabernae, part of one of Roman Hispania's largest cities (roughly 1.2 sq km).

building:840019989.desc: Part of the Sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia, this Π-shaped stoa, built in the 420s BC, housed dining rooms for ritual feasting and votive offerings to Artemis, protector of childbirth and girls' initiation rites.

building:851289754.desc: Thasos's agora developed from the 6th century BC as the city's political and religious center, ringed by stoas and housing temples of Zeus Agoraios and the deified athlete-hero Theagenes; excavated by the French School of Athens since 1911.

building:856482651.desc: Roman theatre of ancient Hatria, built in the 1st century AD under Augustus with a cavea about 70m across and capacity near 10,000; its remains were rediscovered in 1993 during demolition of a former orphanage building.

building:865005128.desc: Theatre of Gallo-Roman Decempagi, revealed only by aerial photography in 1981; an unusually large flat-ground cavea roughly 117m in diameter, estimated to have held up to 16,000, forming part of a major sanctuary complex at the site.

building:891801761.desc: Not a theater: this is the open courtyard (about 30x40m) northwest of the Minoan palace at Malia, which substituted for a formal theatral area since the flat terrain precluded one; its perimeter foundations once carried tiered seating for spectators.

building:899795549.desc: Known as the South Theater, this odeon at Hadrian's Villa seated about 1,100 in a 55m marble-and-mosaic cavea; connected to the rest of the imperial complex by underground service tunnels, built under Hadrian (r. 117-138).

building:908291906.desc: Coordinates place this site at Henchir-Boucha (ancient Tubyza), not Thibiuca proper; its chief spectacle building was a large Roman circus, and its bishop Felix was executed under Diocletian c. 304 AD for refusing to surrender Christian scriptures.

building:932787931.desc: Built in the late 2nd century BC and enlarged under Rome to seat about 3,500, Kourion's theater was converted into a beast-fighting arena in the early 3rd century AD; excavated from 1933 by the University of Pennsylvania.

building:941736668.desc: Winter residence of the Georgian kings in Imereti, expanded into a large domed complex under George III (r. 1156–1184) and favored by Queen Tamar; rebel nobles briefly crowned Yuri Bogolyubsky here in a failed 1191 coup against her.

building:945740227.desc: Suasa's horseshoe-shaped forum, built in the earlier 1st century BC and later monumentalized, was lined on three sides by tabernae opening onto a portico; excavated by the University of Bologna since 1987.

building:95066748.desc: This elliptical amphitheater at the Adriatic town of Gnatia (Egnazia) has walls in opus Africanum masonry from the Julio-Claudian period; scholars still debate whether it staged games or served as a market.

building:958299306.desc: A rock-cut theater built into the hillside at Stymphalos, a planned Classical Arcadian city excavated since 1982 and the legendary site of Herakles' sixth labor, the Stymphalian birds.

building:966420455.desc: In the forum of Luna, a Roman colony founded in 177 BC on the Etruria–Liguria frontier, an inscription of 155 BC found in 1851 honors M. Claudius Marcellus for his triumph over the Ligurians and Apuani.

building:971703785.desc: Discovered in 1998 at the Çömlektepe tumulus in Vize, this is actually a 2nd-century AD Roman theater — the only one known in ancient Thrace — with marble seating for about 4,000 and a monumental female statue now in Kirklareli Museum.

building:975871574.desc: Thugga's forum, beside the Capitol, replaced the earlier Numidian agora at the city's center; little survives above ground, though the site — preserving Punic, Numidian and Roman phases together — was UNESCO-listed in 1997.

building:982593692.desc: Civic sanctuary of the Attic deme Ikarion, birthplace of tragedian Thespis and host of the winter Rural Dionysia; its theater, choregic monument, and temple site were excavated by the American School from 1888-89.

building:983828927.desc: Hellenistic theater at Alinda in Caria, built early 2nd century BC into the acropolis hillside with 35 rows seating roughly 5,000 spectators; renovated under Augustus.

place:dare-20255.desc: The Théâtre romain des Câteliers near Les Andelys (Noyers-sur-Andelys, Eure), discovered by Léon Coutil in 1927; at 120m diameter it ranks among the ten largest theaters of Roman Gaul, built 1st-2nd century AD.

place:pl-109016.desc: Ancient Grand grew around a sanctuary of the Gallo-Roman healing god Apollo-Grannus and built the eighth-largest amphitheater in the Roman world (capacity 16,000–20,000); Emperor Caracalla reportedly visited its shrine in 213 AD.

place:pl-177540.desc: Ancient name of the Gallo-Roman town at Entrains-sur-Nohain, attested by a marble itinerary plaque found at Autun in 1840; aerial survey in 1974 revealed a theater about 115m across, among Gaul's largest.

pleiades:802040030.desc: Elite residence of about 90 rooms at Dūr-Katlimmu (Tell Sheikh Hamad), built by Šulmu-šarri, a senior official of Ashurbanipal (r. 668–631 BC), in the late 7th century BC; it outlasted Assyria's fall before burning down in the 6th century BC.

vici:vici-10015.desc: Roman theater on a Treverian sanctuary plateau above the Saar near Kastel-Staadt, excavated 2006-2008; had 36 rows of seating for about 3,500 spectators, rebuilt in stone around 300 AD after an earlier timber phase.

vici:vici-10173.desc: Roman circus of Vienne, built at the end of the 2nd century AD for chariot racing along a roughly 460m track; its spina was marked by 'La Pyramide,' a 23m truncated obelisk on an arched base still standing today.

vici:vici-10202.desc: Roman theater of Aventicum (Avenches), built early in the 2nd century AD with capacity for roughly 11,000 spectators across about 40 rows; converted into a fortified refuge with a ditch in the later 3rd century.

vici:vici-10280.desc: Lutetia's forum, roughly 178 x 89 m and combining a basilica and temple linked by porticoed galleries, was uncovered in 1867–68 during the cutting of Rue Monge; a Vespasianic coin dates construction to c. AD 69–79.

vici:vici-10378.desc: Wooden theater of Nida, Roman civitas capital at Frankfurt-Heddernheim, its seating raised on an earthen bank; excavated in 1928 but now completely destroyed by later urban development.

vici:vici-10380.desc: Long identified as Nida's forum, this central complex is now known from recent excavations to have been a multi-phase religious precinct of at least eleven temple buildings with ritual pits, not a conventional marketplace.

vici:vici-10448.desc: Theater of Lopodunum, capital of the Civitas Ulpia Sueborum Nicrensium after 98 AD; inscribed stone seats naming individual donors and owners survive, now held in museums including the Badisches Landesmuseum Karlsruhe.

vici:vici-10449.desc: The forum and basilica of Lopodunum, capital of the Civitas Ulpia Sueborum Nicrensium, formed by c. AD 220 the largest known Roman building complex in southern Germany, roughly 130 x 84 m.

vici:vici-10516.desc: Built under Vespasian in the 1st century AD, the 20-hectare forum of Philippopolis is the largest Roman forum in Bulgaria; 1971 excavations uncovered an odeon, a library, and an altar to Demeter and Kore.

vici:vici-10517.desc: Amphitheater of Marcianopolis (Devnya, Bulgaria), excavated 1958-61 by Goranka Toncheva; measured about 65 by 58m with 12 rows of seating in 14 sections and two arched gates, including the porta libitinensis.

vici:vici-10518.desc: Amphitheater of Gorsium (Tác), the Pannonian provincial town that served as center of the imperial cult and, after rebuilding as Herculia, provincial capital; accommodated up to about 2,500 spectators.

vici:vici-10525.desc: Amphitheatre of Brigetio, a Danube-frontier legionary and civilian settlement (Komárom-Szőny, Hungary) that became a municipium around 205 AD; a 2009 epigraphic study argues the town had two separate amphitheatres, military and civic.

vici:vici-10604.desc: Roman theatre built under Augustus c. 40-30 BC, one of the earliest stone theatres in Gaul, dedicated to Apollo; seated roughly 8,000 and yielded the Venus of Arles, found in pieces in 1651.

vici:vici-10605.desc: Forum of Roman Arelate, laid out around 30-20 BC at the crossing of the cardo and decumanus. A 1951 find of a marble copy of Augustus's clipeus virtutis (26 BC) confirms its imperial-cult role; its cryptoporticus survives beneath Place du Forum.

vici:vici-10608.desc: Roman circus of Arles, built in 149 AD on the Rhône bank on thousands of wooden piles; 450m long with tiered seating for up to 20,000, used for chariot races. Its spina obelisk now stands in Arles' Place de la République.

vici:vici-10638.desc: Theatre of Gisacum, the vast Gallo-Roman sanctuary town near Évreux; among the largest in Roman Gaul at c. 106m diameter, holding several thousand spectators, its entrance aligned on the adjoining temple's axis.

vici:vici-10693.desc: Gallo-Roman arena-theatre at Thénac ('Les Arènes'), near Saintes; likely built under Nero and abandoned in the 3rd century, cavea roughly 84-90m across, part of a rural sanctuary with baths and a sacred spring.

vici:vici-10762.desc: Theatre of Lugdunum Convenarum (Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges), the Roman town traditionally linked to Pompey's 72 BC foundation; its cavea was remodelled around the mid-1st century AD with a large porticus post scaenam.

vici:vici-10763.desc: The macellum (market hall) of Lugdunum Convenarum, built beside the forum, burned and was rebuilt in the 2nd century, then destroyed in the 4th; at 55 by 26 m it is the largest known Roman market building in the western provinces.

vici:vici-10764.desc: The older, simpler of Lugdunum Convenarum's two forums, laid out in the early Augustan period on the hilltop oppidum tied to Pompey's original garrison; a trophy marking Augustus's Pyrenean victories may have stood at this symbolically charged site.

vici:vici-10765.desc: The larger, later forum of Lugdunum Convenarum, built in the lower town as the colony expanded under Augustus and Tiberius, centred on a temple oriented toward the earlier forum.

vici:vici-10793.desc: Palatial complex built at Corduba (Córdoba) around 296–297 AD, attributed to the tetrarch Maximian, centered on a 109-m cryptoporticus and audience hall; rediscovered in 1991 during construction of the AVE railway station and later reused as a Christian cult site.

vici:vici-10800.desc: Forum of Carteia, first Latin colony founded outside Italy (171 BC) at the head of the Bay of Gibraltar; imperial-era remains include tabernae flanking one of Andalusia's largest fora, near baths and a hillside theatre.

vici:vici-10866.desc: Hippodrome (circus) of Antinoöpolis, the city Hadrian founded in 130 AD to honour his drowned favourite Antinous; roughly 307m by 77m, it hosted the Antinoeia games of chariot racing and athletic contests.

vici:vici-10881.desc: Palace built in AD 224 by Ardashir I, founder of the Sasanian Empire, near his new capital Ardashir-Khwarrah (Gor, modern Firuzabad); its brick domes on squinches over square chambers are among the earliest known examples of this technique.

vici:vici-10904.desc: The so-called 'Tomba di Agrippina' at Bauli (Bacoli), long linked in legend to Nero's mother but actually a private theatre-nymphaeum (teatro-ninfeo): an Augustan-era odeion within a seaside villa, later converted into a fountain hall.

vici:vici-10906.desc: Theatre remains on the citadel mound of ancient Beroea (Seleucid refoundation of an older Aramaean settlement), later renamed Aleppo; Roman-period Beroea had a colonnaded east-west avenue and a monumental triple arch, with the citadel serving as its acropolis.

vici:vici-10918.desc: Theatre-temple complex of the Samnite Pentri federal sanctuary at Pietrabbondante (identified in older scholarship with the town Bovianum Vetus), built in the late 2nd century BC with its cavea cut into the hillside below the larger Temple B.

vici:vici-10939.desc: Hierapolis Bambyce (modern Manbij, Syria), hellenized from the Aramaic Manbug and known to Greeks as the 'City of the Sanctuary' for its shrine of the Syrian goddess Atargatis, famously described in Lucian's De Dea Syria; distinct from the Phrygian Hierapolis.

vici:vici-10943.desc: Hellenistic theatre of the Carian port city Kaunos near modern Dalyan, seating roughly 5,000 across nine wedges of 33 rows with eight stairways, overlooking the city's silted harbor.

vici:vici-10959.desc: Lower theatre of Cnidus (Knidos) in Caria, built into the hillside beside a temple of Dionysus and holding an estimated 5,000-6,400 spectators; first surveyed by the Society of Dilettanti's 1812 mission and excavated by Charles Newton in 1857-58.

vici:vici-11040.desc: Built by Constantine I as the Forum Tauri and rebuilt by Theodosius I after 393 on the model of Trajan's Forum in Rome; its 40-50 m triumphal column stood until the late 15th century, with fragments surviving near Beyazit Square, Istanbul.

vici:vici-11050.desc: Roman-era (2nd-century AD) theatre of Gythion, Sparta's ancient port on the Laconian Gulf; excavated from 1891 by Andreas Skias, its marble cavea held twelve rows in five wedges with a front row of backed seats reserved for officials.

vici:vici-11094.desc: Theatre of Perinthus, the Samian colony on the Propontis renamed Heraclea under Diocletian; excavations at modern Marmara Ereğlisi have uncovered the theatre together with public baths and the city's fortification walls.

vici:vici-11100.desc: Theatre of Hierapolis (Phrygia), first built in the 2nd century BC, wrecked by the earthquake of 60 AD, then rebuilt under the Flavians in works lasting until c. 206 AD; among Anatolia's largest theatres, with an estimated capacity of 10,000-12,500.

vici:vici-11143.desc: A Hellenistic theatre cut into the hillside at Limyra in Lycia, built in the 2nd-1st century BC with a horseshoe cavea holding about 8,000; badly damaged in the 141 AD earthquake and rebuilt with funds from the Lycian benefactor Opramoas of Rhodiapolis.

vici:vici-11149.desc: The Ancient Theatre of Ohrid, built in Hellenistic style at the end of the 3rd or start of the 2nd century BC, the only theatre of its kind in North Macedonia; Romans later used it for gladiatorial games and it was a site of Christian executions under Diocletian.

vici:vici-11217.desc: Colonial forum of Tarraco, civic centre of the Roman colony in the lower town; an early Republican square dedicated to the Capitoline Triad was replaced under Tiberius by a larger square fronted by a three-aisled basilica.

vici:vici-11218.desc: Built at the end of the 1st century BC under Augustus near the forum and port of Tarraco, seating about 6,000 spectators over three tiers; part of the UNESCO-listed Archaeological Ensemble of Tárraco.

vici:vici-11220.desc: The forum of the Roman colony at Tarraco, distinct from the provincial forum above it, held a basilica, curia and a Capitoline temple to Jupiter, Juno and Minerva; destroyed by fire in late antiquity and never rebuilt.

vici:vici-11236.desc: Forum of Carthago Nova, excavated on the Molinete hill since 1982, held a temple to the Capitoline Triad, a curia, and the Augusteum, a college devoted to the cult of Augustus; a site museum opened in 2021.

vici:vici-11241.desc: Spain's smallest surviving Roman theatre, carved directly into the bedrock at Pollentia (Alcúdia, Mallorca) in the 1st century AD to seat 1,500-2,000; excavated from 1952 and later reused as a late-Roman burial ground.

vici:vici-11249.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Mytilene on Lesbos (3rd-1st century BC), its cavea over 100m across; Pompey had its design copied to build Rome's first permanent stone theatre, dedicated in 55 BC.

vici:vici-11259.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman theatre at Neapolis (Samaria, modern Nablus), one of the largest in Roman Syria-Palestine with seating for several thousand; discovered by chance in 1978 during water-canal excavations, later converted into a water basin.

vici:vici-11262.desc: Built under Augustus in the early 1st century AD as part of Nicopolis's Actian Games complex commemorating his victory at Actium, with a 92m-diameter cavea seating roughly 5,000; in continuous use into the 3rd century AD.

vici:vici-11277.desc: Forum of the Roman colony identified with Colonia Virtus Iulia Ituci, paved in the 1st century AD under Tiberius; an inscription credits Marco Junio Marcelo with financing the pavement, and its north portico yielded a Julio-Claudian dynastic statue group.

vici:vici-11319.desc: Small basalt-built theatre (capacity around 2,000) at Philippopolis, the Hauran village emperor Philip the Arab (r. 244-249) rebuilt as a colonia; construction halted, unfinished, at his death.

vici:vici-11324.desc: Hellenistic theatre (15 tiers, capacity ~3,000) of ancient Phthiotic Thebes, identified and partly excavated in 1992–93; in the Roman period it was remodeled into an arena for wild-beast fights.

vici:vici-11325.desc: Well-preserved Hellenistic theatre of Pinara in Lycia, built into a hillside in the late 2nd century BC with a capacity of about 3,200; nearly all its stone seating survives intact.

vici:vici-11345.desc: Theatre of ancient Samos near Pythagoreion, on the slope below the sanctuary of Panagia Spiliani; construction is dated to the 4th century BC with Roman-era rebuilding, though little masonry survives today.

vici:vici-11353.desc: Mountain theatre of Selge in Pisidia, built in the 2nd century AD with a 104 m cavea seating roughly 9,000–10,000; later reduced and converted into an arena in the 3rd century.

vici:vici-11403.desc: Rural Gallo-Roman theatre at Les Bardiaux (Arleuf, Morvan), built around the late 2nd century AD with six concentric tiers seating 600–700; discovered in 1971, it likely also hosted public assemblies.

vici:vici-11406.desc: Large Gallo-Roman theatre (110 m diameter, seating up to 8,000–10,000) at the sanctuary of Les Vaux-de-la-Celle, Genainville; sometimes, inconclusively, identified with the Peutinger Table's station of Petromantalum/Petrum Viacum.

vici:vici-11409.desc: Roman theatre of Teanum Sidicinum (Teano), among Italy's largest with an 85 m cavea seating 6,000–7,000; built from the late 2nd century BC, systematically excavated by Werner Johannowsky, 1961–1976.

vici:vici-11413.desc: Despite being labeled 'Thapsus,' this theatre stands at Rusicade (modern Skikda), the largest Roman theatre in Algeria, built under Hadrian; long quarried for stone, only fragments survive today.

vici:vici-11414.desc: Ancient Greek theatre of Thasos, cut into the acropolis hillside; first built early 3rd century BC with a marble stage front dedicated to Dionysus by Lysistratos, later used from the 1st century AD for beast hunts and gladiatorial combat.

vici:vici-11441.desc: Best-preserved structure at Tusculum: a Roman theatre with a 45m cavea and 17.5m orchestra, built early 1st century AD and excavated by Luigi Canina in 1839-40 for Lucien Bonaparte's campaign.

vici:vici-11444.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman hippodrome at Tyre, 480m by 90m and seating 20,000, among the largest and best-preserved in the Roman world; hosted the Heraclia games for Melqart-Heracles every four years.

vici:vici-11449.desc: Roman odeon on the Fourvière hill in Lyon (Lugdunum), built early-mid 2nd century AD with a 73m diameter seating 3,000; one of only two paired odeon-theatre complexes in Roman Gaul, alongside Vienne.

vici:vici-11462.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Vieux-Poitiers (ancient Vetus Pictavis, Naintré), 116m in diameter and seating an estimated 10,000 - larger than the theatre of Orange - hosting drama and gladiatorial combat for two centuries.

vici:vici-11463.desc: Theatre of the Roman vicus Augusti (modern Sidi el-Hani, Tunisia), with a 58m cavea and 22m orchestra; its ruins, with radial vaulted substructures, were first studied by Saladin and Cagnat in 1882.

vici:vici-11524.desc: Stone Roman theatre built AD 161 on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis by Herodes Atticus in memory of his wife Aspasia Annia Regilla; seated about 5,000 and still hosts the Athens Festival today.

vici:vici-11525.desc: Athens's original odeon, built 435 BC by Pericles for the musical contests of the Panathenaea, its square timber roof said to imitate Xerxes's tent; burned during Sulla's siege in 86 BC and later rebuilt.

vici:vici-11528.desc: The smaller of Gadara's two Roman theatres, built of basalt on the acropolis's western slope in the Severan period (193-235 AD) with seating for about 3,000; 1960s excavations uncovered a marble statue of Tyche, now in the Umm Qais museum.

vici:vici-11532.desc: Begun under Septimius Severus around AD 203 and enlarged by Constantine I after 330 as Constantinople's chariot-racing arena and ceremonial center; site of the AD 532 Nika riots, its spina still marked by an Egyptian obelisk and the Serpent Column.

vici:vici-11534.desc: Seat and residence of the Roman governor of Germania Inferior, uncovered by chance beneath Cologne's town hall in 1953; grew to roughly 40,000 m² over four building phases and is the only known praetorium whose name survives in an inscription.

vici:vici-11535.desc: Built 416-418 for the powerful chamberlain Antiochus near the Hippodrome of Constantinople; became imperial property after his fall in 421, and part of it was later converted into the Church of St Euphemia.

vici:vici-11548.desc: Built as a bouleuterion around the 1st century BC and rebuilt as an odeon around AD 160; Strabo calls it a gerontikon, the meeting hall of the city elders, with 12 rows of seats holding roughly 600-700 people.

vici:vici-11555.desc: Roman theatre built in the city founded under Hadrian (who visited the region in AD 125); its cavea, 58 m across with 24 limestone rows, seated about 3,500-4,000. Rediscovered after a 1970s landslide, confirmed as Hadrianopolis by 2002 excavations.

vici:vici-11564.desc: Late Hellenistic or early imperial theatre of Magnesia on the Maeander, left unfinished and only discovered by excavation in 1984; the cavea survives but no trace remains of its probably wooden stage building.

vici:vici-11565.desc: Built around 370 BC by the architect Polycleitus the Younger for the newly founded federal capital of the Arcadian League; Pausanias called it the largest theatre in Greece, holding up to about 20,000 and doubling as the League's assembly hall.

vici:vici-11571.desc: Roman odeon of Patras built in the first half of the 2nd century AD, praised by Pausanias as second only to the Odeon of Herodes Atticus; destroyed by fire in the late 3rd century, rediscovered in 1889, and restored in 1959-61.

vici:vici-11584.desc: Mactaris' second, porticoed forum (about 1,500 sq m) is entered through the Arch of Trajan, dedicated in AD 116 to mark the city's changed status; the arch was later incorporated into Byzantine-era fortifications.

vici:vici-11591.desc: Theatre of Uthina (Oudhna), near the Numidian-Roman colony's forum and capitol; unlike the city's well-preserved 16,000-seat amphitheatre, it survives only as a shallow depression in the hillside.

vici:vici-11593.desc: Hellenistic theatre on Pergamon's upper acropolis, built c. 225-200 BC and enlarged under Eumenes II; the steepest theatre of the ancient world, with 78 rows seating about 10,000, fronted by a 247m colonnaded terrace and the Temple of Dionysus.

vici:vici-11598.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Les Bouchauds (ancient Germanicomagus) in Charente, cut into a hillside beside a sanctuary; at 105.6m diameter the largest theatre in Aquitaine, seating 5,000-6,000, discovered in 1865 and protected as a historic monument since 1881.

vici:vici-11615.desc: Three U-shaped vaulted galleries built around 30-10 BC to level uneven ground beneath the forum of Roman Arelate (Arles); used for storage and structural support, they are among the best-preserved Roman substructures, part of the city's UNESCO listing.

vici:vici-11671.desc: Circus built by Tiberius in AD 14 at Bovillae, ancestral town of the Julian family, in honor of the deified Augustus; measured c. 337 x 69m, with yearly imperial-cult games instituted there from AD 16.

vici:vici-11713.desc: Roman amphitheatre of Tarentum (Taranto), southwest of the Chiesa del Carmine, possibly built over one of the city's two unexcavated Greek theatres; no remains are visible today.

vici:vici-11739.desc: Imperial residence built by co-emperor Maximian after Diocletian made Mediolanum capital of the Western Roman Empire in 286 AD; excavations near Via Brisa since 1951 have uncovered walls, mosaic floors and baths of the vast complex.

vici:vici-11759.desc: Small Roman theatre within Pergamon's Asclepieion healing sanctuary, seating about 3,500; built into the sanctuary's northwest corner, with performances forming part of the complex's ritual therapy during its Hadrianic-era remodeling.

vici:vici-11768.desc: Stadium of Ephesus, first laid out by Lysimachus in the 3rd century BC and rebuilt for chariot races, athletics, and beast/gladiator fights under Nero; U-shaped, 229 x 28m, its southern seating cut into the slope of Mount Pion.

vici:vici-11769.desc: Small covered theater built c. 150 AD by the wealthy Ephesian Publius Vedius Antoninus and his wife Flavia Papiana; seated roughly 1,400–1,500 and served both as a concert hall (odeon) and as the city's council chamber (bouleuterion).

vici:vici-11776.desc: Roman stadium of Philippopolis, built in the early 2nd century AD under Hadrian, about 240 m long and seating up to 30,000; its curved northern end (sphendone) survives exposed beneath Plovdiv's main pedestrian street.

vici:vici-11811.desc: Forum of Roman Tipasa (76m by 43m) with porticoes on three sides and a capitolium on the fourth, alongside a curia and civil basilica; part of the coastal city, UNESCO World Heritage-listed since 1982.

vici:vici-11826.desc: Forum, about 55m square with a Corinthian portico, of Thuburbo Majus, which was elevated to colony status as Colonia Julia Aurelia Commoda under Commodus in AD 188; its Capitolium to Jupiter, Juno and Minerva was dedicated in AD 168.

vici:vici-11840.desc: Stadium of Aphrodisias, built in the later 1st century AD, c. 270 x 60 m with 30 tiers of seating for up to 30,000; considered the best-preserved stadium of the ancient world and used for athletic and artistic contests.

vici:vici-11843.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Assos, built around the late 4th–3rd century BC on a slope overlooking the Aegean with views to Lesbos; seated roughly 5,000 and gained a two-storey stage building in the Roman period.

vici:vici-11846.desc: Greco-Roman theatre of Myra, badly damaged in the 141 AD earthquake and rebuilt with funds from the Lycian benefactor Opramoas of Rhodiapolis; seated up to about 12,000, making it the largest theatre in Lycia.

vici:vici-11879.desc: Theatre at the Gallo-Roman sanctuary complex of Cherré (Aubigné-Racan, Sarthe), a 1st–3rd century AD rural cult and market center excavated since 1977 that also included two temples, baths, an aqueduct, and a forum.

vici:vici-11882.desc: Market-forum of the rural Gallo-Roman sanctuary complex at Cherré near Aubigné-Racan, which also had a theatre, temples and baths, built from the late 1st century AD and excavated since 1977; apparently without permanent habitation.

vici:vici-11915.desc: Roman theatre built by Herod the Great (37–4 BC) at Caesarea Maritima, among the earliest Roman-style theatres in the region; seated roughly 3,500–4,000 and, after restoration, is still used for performances today.

vici:vici-11916.desc: Monumental hippodrome/stadium built by Herod the Great, dedicated with the city in 10/9 BC and roughly 315 m long; hosted the quadrennial Actian Games (chariot racing, athletics, gladiatorial combat) and was later remodeled as an amphitheater.

vici:vici-11918.desc: Theatre serving Anderitum, Gallo-Roman capital of the Gabali civitas at Javols; built between the 1st and 3rd centuries AD, possibly a mixed theatre-amphitheatre, with only the perimeter wall and foundations surviving.

vici:vici-11924.desc: The rock-hewn theatre (or odeion) of Roman Canatha (Qanawat) in the Hauran, cut into the wadi slope with nine tiers of basalt seating around a 19m orchestra; likely 3rd-century, possibly cult-related.

vici:vici-11932.desc: Ancient Greek theatre of Mantinea in Arcadia, built into the city's agora complex after its 371 BC reconstruction following destruction by Sparta; unusually raised entirely on an artificial mound rather than a hillside.

vici:vici-11933.desc: Small theatre cut into bedrock at Melli (Kocaaliler) in Pisidia; a 2000 Pisidia Survey Project excavation suggested a Severan-era (early 3rd-century AD) construction date, though the city's ancient name remains unknown.

vici:vici-11972.desc: A rare rural Romano-British theatre-sanctuary near Faversham, Kent, excavated 2007-2013; a circular 'cockpit-style' auditorium set within a religious complex with two temple enclosures and a sacred spring, apparently used for ceremonial gatherings.

vici:vici-11974.desc: Large elliptical Gallo-Roman amphitheatre of Rotomagus (Rouen), built late in the 2nd century AD and among the largest in Gaul at roughly 130 by 120 metres; abandoned by the 4th century and later buried under Philip Augustus's castle.

vici:vici-11986.desc: The Small Roman Theatre of Pula, built in the 1st century AD on the hillside below the citadel; seated an estimated 4,000-5,000 for drama and recitations, with 2011-2022 excavations restoring the site.

vici:vici-11991.desc: The only known Roman-era theatre in Thrace, built in the 2nd century AD at Bizye (Vize); discovered in 1998, its marble cavea seated some 4,000, with stage reliefs now held in Kırklareli Museum.

vici:vici-12001.desc: Built in AD 79 and dedicated to Vespasian and Titus, funded privately by the citizen Nikostratos; at roughly 285 x 70 m this was one of Roman Anatolia's largest stadiums, seating up to 25,000 for games.

vici:vici-12005.desc: A 1st-century AD Roman theatre built into a hillside at Hierapolis Kastabala in Cilicia, seating roughly 5,000 across ima and summa cavea; excavations have recovered 36 theatrical stage masks, including five found in 2025.

vici:vici-12007.desc: A small, well-preserved covered odeon/bouleuterion at Anemurium in Rough Cilicia, excavated with the rest of the city (UBC excavations, 1965-1993), seating roughly 900-1,100 for council meetings or performances.

vici:vici-12008.desc: Anemurium's main Roman theatre, a semicircular structure about 60 m in diameter, built in the 3rd century AD at the height of the city's prosperity.

vici:vici-12019.desc: A Roman-period stadium at the foot of the Sardis acropolis, south of the gymnasium, with a U-shaped, double-sided cavea built against vaulted substructures on the slope.

vici:vici-12020.desc: Theater built at Sardis during the Hellenistic period as part of the city's conversion into a Greek-style polis; it stood alongside a stadium among Sardis's Roman-era civic monuments.

vici:vici-12057.desc: Roman theater at Shuni (ancient Kfar Shumi) built with an adjoining semicircular pool used for the Graeco-Syrian Maiumas water festival honoring Dionysus and Aphrodite; excavated from 1986 by E. Shenhav for the JNF.

vici:vici-12877.desc: The only known Roman theater in Noricum, built in the 2nd century AD with a semicircular cavea of 35 m radius and a 32.5 m stage, near Virunum's amphitheater; excavated 1890s–1931 and again since the 1990s.

vici:vici-12885.desc: Small roofed odeon at Nicopolis ad Istrum (founded by Trajan c. 102–106) seating up to about 350; its stone cavea was supported on arches sheltering eleven shops, separated from the semicircular orchestra by iron-braced stone blocks.

vici:vici-13588.desc: Roman amphitheater at Tomis, arena c. 60x40 m within a complex up to 100x80 m and 20–25 m tall, discovered in 1980; a 2nd–3rd century AD section found in 1988–89 was preserved beneath the Ibis hotel.

vici:vici-13911.desc: Forum of Roman Aquileia (colony founded 181 BC), monumentalized in the 1st century AD with porticoes carved with Medusa and Jupiter Ammon reliefs; its location was lost after antiquity and rediscovered only in the 1930s.

vici:vici-13914.desc: Roman circus built around 293 AD under Maximian near his Aquileia residence for chariot racing; still in use in 425, when the usurper John (Johannes) was executed there on Valentinian III's orders.

vici:vici-14029.desc: Forum of Ulpia Traiana Sarmizegetusa, capital of Roman Dacia founded c. 108-110 AD under Trajan; a monumental gated courtyard with emperor statues led to a basilica (tribunal) and curia where the city council met.

vici:vici-14461.desc: A wall with buttresses at Arae Flaviae (Rottweil), near a sanctuary and blocks resembling seating stones, has been tentatively identified as a theater on the strength of geomagnetic surveys showing a monumental complex, though the evidence remains inconclusive.
