vici:vici-45510.desc: Its remains lie beneath Écija's 19th-century bullring; at roughly 130 x 107 m with an 80-arch exterior facade modeled on Rome's Flavian Amphitheatre, it likely shared architects with the amphitheatre at nearby Italica.

vici:vici-45568.desc: Unlike the city's well-preserved Roman theatre, the amphitheatre of Caesaraugusta has never been conclusively located; a long-standing theory placing it beneath the Hotel Palafox site has not been confirmed by excavation.

vici:vici-46394.desc: Missua (modern Sidi Daoud, Cap Bon) was a Roman port town of Africa Proconsularis with trade ties to Ostia and a Christian bishopric attested from 484 AD; its theatre remains stand near the ancient harbor and breakwater.

vici:vici-46797.desc: Megaron-form palace complex on Midea's lower acropolis terraces, built in the Mycenaean palatial period (LH IIIB2) and destroyed by fire around 1200 BC along with the rest of the citadel, then rebuilt on a smaller scale with a central colonnade in LH IIIC.

vici:vici-48059.desc: First identified via 2016 LiDAR and georadar survey north of Castulo's walled Iberian-Roman city (Linares, Jaén); its existence had long been suspected but its exact location was only pinpointed through this remote-sensing work.

vici:vici-48683.desc: A largely vanished Roman amphitheatre of Barcino; no confirmed remains have been excavated, though archaeologist Jordina Sales hypothesizes a location near Santa Maria del Mar based on the district's oval street pattern.

vici:vici-49118.desc: A circus at the Roman municipium of Singilia Barba, identified through epigraphic evidence and geophysical survey near the city's buried theatre (capacity c. 2,200), part of a monumental zone that also included a forum.

vici:vici-49794.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre of Iatinum (Meaux), built in the 1st–2nd century AD; a 1993–94 rescue excavation uncovered cavea substructures spanning over 150 m, and an inscription credits its funding to a local notable, Orgetorix.

vici:vici-49922.desc: Gallo-Roman amphitheatre of Vindunum (Le Mans), capital of the Aulerci Cenomani; rediscovered in 1791 by architect Daudin during urban works and destroyed the following year when the Jacobins promenade was laid out.

vici:vici-50043.desc: Structure at the Roman town of Contributa Iulia Ugultunia (Medina de las Torres, Badajoz), identified in 2016 via geophysical survey as an amphitheatre, one of the first found this way in Iberia; the site preserves an unusually intact street grid.

vici:vici-50056.desc: Circus of the Roman colony Augusta Firma Astigi (Écija), built outside the walls between the Via Augusta and the road to Augusta Emerita; a semicircular structure near the presumed prima meta was clarified by 2023–2024 excavations.

vici:vici-50062.desc: Roman circus of ancient Consabura, confirmed by geophysical survey and 2017 excavations; an 18th-century plan recorded it at roughly 370 x 80 m with a capacity near 10,000, one of only about a dozen archaeologically documented circuses in Roman Hispania.

vici:vici-50161.desc: Forum of Apta Julia (modern Apt), founded on Julius Caesar's order in 45 BC as a colony on the Via Domitia; excavations beneath the town revealed the forum, a basilica, and the decumanus and cardo maximus.

vici:vici-50599.desc: Small horseshoe-shaped Nabataean-Roman theater (39 m wide, 11 rows seating 500-800) cut into a gully at Sabra near Petra; built in the 1st century AD with a water-channel system beneath the seating, remaining in use into the late Roman period.

vici:vici-50642.desc: The roughly seven-hectare residence of the governor of Pannonia Inferior on Óbuda's Hajógyári-sziget island near Aquincum, first built under Hadrian in the 2nd century AD with mosaic floors and underfloor heating, rebuilt after a fire in the early 3rd century, and abandoned by the late 3rd century.

vici:vici-50768.desc: The unfinished Roman theater of Palmyra, built in the Severan 2nd century AD with a cavea of eleven sections/twelve rows never completed; buried in sand for centuries, it was excavated and restored in the 1950s, and its stage facade was destroyed by ISIL in 2017.

vici:vici-52210.desc: A mound within the Kassite capital of Dur-Kurigalzu near Baghdad; Iraqi excavations in 1992, 1993 and 2001 uncovered Late Kassite and Parthian-Sasanian remains, painted wall plaster, a cylinder seal, and cuneiform tablets dated to king Nazi-Maruttash.

vici:vici-52622.desc: Roman Forum of Thessaloniki, a two-terraced complex with double-storey stoas, baths and a small theatre used for gladiatorial games; found by accident in the 1960s, excavated in the 1970s-80s, and used into the 6th century.

vici:vici-52623.desc: Built in the early 4th century AD under the Tetrarchy, this roughly 450 x 95 m racecourse was the site of the 390 AD massacre in which Theodosius I's Gothic troops killed thousands of citizens after a riot; fragments survive beneath the modern Hippodromiou district.

vici:vici-52625.desc: Agora of ancient Korkyra at Palaiopolis, Corfu, excavated 1936-1955 alongside the Early Christian basilica of Agia Kerkyra; the settlement was founded by Corinthian colonists (led by Chersicrates) in the 8th century BC.

vici:vici-53620.desc: Forum of Segóbriga, built c. 15 BC under Augustus and partly funded by Proculus Spantamicus, whose name in bronze letters fronted a monument bearing the emperor's statue; a rectangular plaza framed by a colonnaded portico.

vici:vici-53629.desc: Forum of Valeria, among the best-preserved on the Iberian Meseta, built mainly in the 1st century AD with a basilica, curia/exedra, cryptoporticus, monumental staircase, shops and the roughly 100m-long nymphaeum fountain.

vici:vici-53785.desc: Forum of Complutum (Alcalá de Henares): a monumental 3rd-century complex with basilica, curia, baths and porticoed shops (tabernae), built over an earlier 1st-century forum; the city flourished until the 6th-7th century.

vici:vici-54676.desc: Cross-shaped reception hall at the core of Shapur I's palace at Bishapur, flanked by 'mosaic halls' whose Sasanian floor mosaics of banqueting, dancing and portrait figures show Roman artistic influence; excavated by Roman Ghirshman for the Louvre, 1935-1941.

vici:vici-54715.desc: A largely unexcavated Achaemenid structure east of the Palace of Xerxes at Persepolis, known chiefly from scattered relief fragments (including a small winged-monster relief) and never fully cleared or published.

vici:vici-54817.desc: Hellenistic palace of Dura-Europos's military governor (the strategos), built within a fortified 'redoubt' on high ground above the Euphrates in the 2nd century BC; its state rooms open onto a colonnaded courtyard about 11 by 11.5 metres, with the Temple of Zeus Megistos immediately to the south.

vici:vici-54922.desc: Civic square of Glanum, a Gallo-Greek/Gallo-Roman town below the Alpilles that grew from a Celtic sanctuary to the healing spring-god Glanis; monumentalized under Augustus, excavated from 1921, abandoned after 3rd-century raids.

vici:vici-54949.desc: Geophysical surveys announced in 2023 revealed likely remains of a Roman circus east of Italica's Hadrianic quarter — an exceptionally large structure some 532 m long with capacity for up to 80,000 spectators; a 1799 mosaic of chariot-race scenes and racehorse graffiti in the city's theater had earlier hinted at its existence.

vici:vici-55005.desc: A well-preserved Late Bronze Age building at Tall al-'Umayri excavated by the Madaba Plains Project, first called a 'palace' but reinterpreted as a temple after standing stones, chalices, lamps and figurines were found; its walls survive over three metres high.

vici:vici-55079.desc: Built around AD 15–45, among the earliest amphitheaters raised in Roman Gaul alongside those of Lyon and Saintes, it stood north of Durocortorum near the road to Boulogne-sur-Mer; its remains had vanished by the mid-19th century and were rediscovered in 2007–08 during tramway construction.

vici:vici-55083.desc: Summer residence of the Arsacid kings of Armenia within the Garni fortress, built under Tiridates I in the 1st century CE; its bath house preserves a Greek-inscribed mosaic of Oceanus and Thetis, the only pagan-era mosaic to survive in Armenia.

vici:vici-55148.desc: The colonnaded porticus post scaenam behind Leptis Magna's theater enclosed a temple to the Dei Augusti; built by Iddibal of the Tapapius family (who also built the theater itself) and dedicated by the proconsul Quintus Marcius Barea.

vici:vici-55151.desc: Leptis Magna's original forum (Forum Vetus), predating the later Severan Forum, flanked by temples to Liber Pater, Rome and Augustus (built 8 BC-19 AD), and Hercules — a key monument of the city's Augustan-era Romanization.

vici:vici-55156.desc: Forum of Roman Philadelphia (Amman), one of the largest public squares of the Roman world at roughly 100 x 50 m, built in the 2nd century AD facing the Roman theatre and bounded by the Seil Amman stream.

vici:vici-55220.desc: Upper terrace above Petra's colonnaded street, reached by a monumental staircase past a Trajanic-period arch; part of the city's Nabataean-to-Roman civic/commercial core, in use from the 1st century BC into the 7th century AD.

vici:vici-55287.desc: Bishop's residence at Heraclea Lyncestis, excavated 1970–75; its four private chambers, floored with Early Christian figural mosaics, formed part of the early Byzantine episcopal complex alongside the Great Basilica.

vici:vici-55320.desc: The outermost of three courtyards in Darius I's palace at Susa, begun c. 510 BCE and excavated by Jane and Marcel Dieulafoy in the 1880s, part of the residential quarter west of the famous Apadana.

vici:vici-55321.desc: Central courtyard of Darius I's Susa palace, where archaeologists dug through into pre-Achaemenid occupation levels beneath the Persian remains during 19th–20th century excavations.

vici:vici-55322.desc: Innermost courtyard of the Susa palace, often identified with the 'inner court' before the king's gate in the biblical Book of Esther, giving access to the King's Hall.

vici:vici-55323.desc: The king's audience chamber south of the palace courtyards at Susa, where Darius I and his successors received visitors on the royal throne; excavated with the adjoining Apadana in the 1880s–1900s.

vici:vici-55721.desc: The main royal palace (Building M 750) of the Kushite capital at Meroë, excavated by John Garstang in the early 20th century and again in 2004–2007, revealing courtyard architecture and an earlier mudbrick palace beneath dating to c. 900 BCE.

vici:vici-55798.desc: The Provincial Forum of Tarraco, seat of the imperial cult and assembly of Hispania Citerior, built from 73 AD under Vespasian on two terraces, its lower square measuring about 318 x 175 m; part of the UNESCO-listed Tarraco ensemble.

vici:vici-55892.desc: The council house of Hellenistic Sia, a Pisidian city near Bucak founded c. 2nd century BC; it is the only building at the site securely predating Roman rule, when three temples, an agora with Doric stoa, and Byzantine basilicas were added.

vici:vici-55971.desc: Amphitheatre (about 80 by 45 m) of the Roman colony of Sinuessa, founded 296 BC near modern Mondragone; the town was noted for its thermal springs and Falernian wine before coastal erosion later submerged part of the settlement.

vici:vici-56005.desc: Residence of the entu-priestess of the moon god Nanna at Ur; excavated by Leonard Woolley in 1927, it yielded the alabaster disk of Enheduanna, priestess-daughter of Sargon of Akkad and history's earliest named author.

vici:vici-56023.desc: Part of Kourion's fifth-century Episcopal precinct, seat of the city's Christian bishop alongside its basilica and baptistery; excavated in the 1930s-70s. Occupation ended after Arab raids destroyed the site in 649.

vici:vici-56024.desc: Agora of Kourion, rebuilt in the late 2nd-early 3rd century AD over an earlier Hellenistic public building, ringed by marble-columned porticoes and giving access to a large nymphaeum and bath complex on its northwest side.

vici:vici-56141.desc: The cryptoporticus of Aeminium (Coimbra): a 1st/2nd-century AD underground gallery of vaulted corridors built to level the site for the city's forum, preserved intact beneath the Bishop's Palace, today the Machado de Castro National Museum.

vici:vici-57005.desc: Stadium of ancient Sicyon, lying just west of the city's theatre and shaped largely by the hillside; unlike fully excavated Greek stadia, seating did not run the full length of the track, with one end built up on fill.

vici:vici-57411.desc: Marketplace of Porolissum's civilian settlement, a Dacian frontier town and customs station on the Roman limes; excavations there recovered coins, native brooches, and raw Baltic amber marking a branch of the Amber Road into the empire.

vici:vici-58125.desc: The 'Palazzo Imperiale' at Portus, a three-storey imperial residence of nearly 7.5 acres built late under Trajan or early under Hadrian between the Claudian and Trajanic harbour basins, likely used by the port's procurator and visiting emperors.

vici:vici-58131.desc: A market building within Portus, the artificial harbour Claudius built for Rome's grain supply near Ostia and Trajan later enlarged with a hexagonal inner basin and vast warehouse complex.

vici:vici-58188.desc: Athens' Panathenaic Stadium was built c. 330 BC under Lycurgus for the Panathenaic Games, rebuilt entirely in Pentelic marble by Herodes Atticus by 144 AD, and restored again for the first modern Olympics in 1896 as the world's only all-marble stadium.

vici:vici-58426.desc: Amphitheatre of Trea, a Roman municipium on a Picene/Sabine hilltop near modern Treia that flourished from Augustus to Antoninus Pius; its oval outline is still traceable as a crop mark near the Santuario del Santissimo Crocifisso.

vici:vici-58447.desc: Geophysical survey in 2010–11 near the Roman fort Iciniacum (Theilenhofen) on the Raetian Limes revealed a c. 75 m theatre-shaped structure beside a large plaza interpreted as a forum, probably temporary buildings for a single major event.

vici:vici-58497.desc: The Domus Tiberiana on the Palatine's northwest slope, begun under Tiberius and enlarged by his successors; damaged in the 64 AD fire, absorbed into Nero's Domus Aurea, and later the Antonine emperors' main residence.

vici:vici-58541.desc: A curving timber structure identified as a possible amphitheatre stood in the civilian settlement outside the east rampart of Inveresk Roman fort near Musselburgh, occupied from Antoninus Pius's AD 139/140 invasion; if confirmed, the empire's northernmost amphitheatre.

vici:vici-58553.desc: A possible amphitheatre with only scant surface traces, lying east of the mid-1st-century Roman auxiliary fort at Forden Gaer, which guarded a crossing of the River Severn near Montgomery, Wales.

vici:vici-58567.desc: Known only from inscriptions (CIL XIII 4132, 198 CE, and a text of 253 CE) recording that Lucius Ammatius Gamburio endowed the theatre of Beda (Bitburg) with 50,000 denarii and funded annual games, probably for the Floralia; the building itself has never been located.

vici:vici-58751.desc: The so-called 'Palazzo Imperiale' in Ostia's Regio I, a multi-purpose complex of baths, shops and a mithraeum built mainly under the Antonine and Severan emperors; a lead pipe naming Trajan's niece Matidia ties it to the imperial family, not an emperor's own residence.

vici:vici-58763.desc: Amphitheatre of Roman Hatria (Adria); its buried foundations near the Canalbianco were found in rescue excavations and later traced by ground-penetrating radar, its superstructure having been stripped away in the Middle Ages.

vici:vici-59657.desc: Site of a Roman fortified camp at Liffremont; 19th-century finds included gold, silver and bronze imperial coins and the 3rd-century marble 'Venus of Liffremont' (now in Rouen's Museum of Antiquities); the location of a theatre was also identified here, though it remains unexcavated.

vici:vici-60162.desc: The forum of Augusta Bagiennorum, chief town of the Ligurian Bagienni founded near the end of the 1st century BC; excavated in 1941, it revealed a paved square 36 by 116 m flanked by porticoes with shop and office rooms.

vici:vici-60167.desc: Small theatre in Theveste (Tébessa, Algeria), built after the city was refounded as a base for Legio III Augusta; the colony also has a 3rd-century triumphal arch of Caracalla and a 4th-century amphitheatre.

vici:vici-60175.desc: 1st-century AD theatre of the Roman town of Libarna (Piedmont), with a 22-arch external ambulatory on sandstone-based pillars; the ruins, later damaged by farming and two railway lines, sit beside the town's amphitheatre and baths.

vici:vici-60254.desc: Josephus records that Herod the Great funded a theatre at Damascus, one of several benefactions (gymnasia, temples, colonnades) to Greek cities outside his own kingdom; the structure's physical remains have not been securely located.

vici:vici-60356.desc: Not a built amphitheatre but a natural rock formation ('Pietrele Scrise') near Casian in Dobrogea, bearing two Greek boundary inscriptions ('Oroi Kasianon') dated to the 2nd-3rd century AD and first deciphered by Vasile Pârvan in 1912-13.

vici:vici-60813.desc: The forum of Virunum, capital of the Roman province of Noricum founded under Claudius on the Zollfeld plain; it lay toward the eastern end of the grid, flanked by a Capitolium and enclosed on three sides by a double colonnade.

vici:vici-60834.desc: The commander's residence within the 23-hectare Roman legionary camp at Anreppen on the Lippe, built around AD 4 for Tiberius's Germanic campaigns; at roughly 3,400 m2 it was the largest praetorium of the Lippe camps, abandoned by AD 9.

vici:vici-61062.desc: Part of the Ruin Garden at Savaria (Szombathely), capital of Pannonia Superior, excavated from 1932; its mosaic-paved remains are traditionally identified as the governor's palace, where visiting emperors including Septimius Severus reportedly stayed.

vici:vici-61066.desc: At Salla (Zalalövő) on the Amber Road, a Roman camp founded in the early 1st century AD grew into a municipium under Hadrian in 124; in the early 4th century its site was rebuilt in stone as a guest house for high-status travelers.

vici:vici-61475.desc: Hypothesized Roman circus at Nemausus (Nîmes), proposed by archaeologist Richard Pellé from imported sand deposits found near Rue Cité Foulc matching the arena floor; if real, it would postdate the amphitheatre, built in Beaucaire limestone in the later 2nd century AD.

vici:vici-61516.desc: The forum of Gigthis, a Phoenician-founded trading post on Tunisia's Gulf of Gabès that became a Roman municipium under Antoninus Pius; a colonnaded, limestone-paved esplanade fronting a temple, at its height in the 2nd–3rd centuries CE.

vici:vici-61640.desc: 2nd-century AD amphitheatre at the 'Villa of the Antonines' near Genzano di Roma (ancient Lanuvium), a family estate of the Antonine dynasty; possibly the arena where Commodus killed wild beasts and earned the nickname 'Roman Hercules'.

vici:vici-61935.desc: A Gallo-Roman theatre discovered in 1993 near the sanctuary of Blicquy, the only known Roman theatre building in Belgium; timber-and-stone construction about 70m across, estimated to have seated 5,000-6,000.

vici:vici-62026.desc: Theatre of the Illyrian hill city of Dimale (Dimallum), built amid Hellenistic urban expansion in the 3rd-2nd century BC; the fortified city was captured by Rome under L. Aemilius Paullus in 219 BC after a seven-day siege.

vici:vici-62184.desc: A hippodrome tentatively identified in 1993 from Roman-period brick pillars at Pelusium (Tell el-Farama), one of only two hippodromes known from Roman Egypt, alongside Antinoöpolis.

vici:vici-62185.desc: A large imperial-period amphitheatre at Pelusium (Tell el-Farama), excavated from 1992 and linked to a drainage system serving the surrounding city; identified by archaeologist Ahmed el-Tabai.

vici:vici-62189.desc: A modest wooden amphitheatre with a circular palisade ditch built in the southern corner of the timber-and-earth Roman fort at Unterkirchberg (Illerkirchberg) on the Raetian Danube limes, founded under Claudius.

vici:vici-62216.desc: The Roman circus of Carteia (San Roque, Cadiz), whose spina and meta secunda were documented in 2017 excavations, confirming a chariot-racing track at this Phoenician-Carthaginian-Roman port city on the Bay of Gibraltar.

vici:vici-62218.desc: The Roman circus of Valentia (Valencia), built in the 2nd century AD, measured roughly 350 x 70m and could seat up to 10,000; first identified in 1987, its remains lie beneath several buildings in Ciutat Vella.

vici:vici-62225.desc: Site of Roman Consabura, predecessor of Consuegra; a rare Roman circus — one of only about a dozen securely attested in Hispania — was excavated here in December 2017 near Viriato Park.

vici:vici-62327.desc: The 'Palatial Complex' at Kerkenes Dağ, entered through a monumental gateway in the 7 km circuit wall of this Iron Age Anatolian city, the largest pre-Hellenistic settlement on the plateau; built and occupied for about a century before its destruction c. 550 BC.

vici:vici-62499.desc: Oval Roman amphitheatre of Divona Cadurcorum (Cahors), c. 110 by 90 metres, built in the 1st century AD; its buried remains were found beneath Place Gambetta during car-park construction and opened to visitors in 2009.

vici:vici-62501.desc: The Roman theatre of Augustodunum (Autun), built into a hillside in the later 1st century AD, spanned some 148 metres and held up to c. 14,000 — the second-largest theatre in the Roman world after Pompey's in Rome.

vici:vici-62664.desc: The forum of Althiburos (Henchir Medeina), a Berber-Numidian settlement that became a Roman municipium under Hadrian; excavations from 1908 uncovered the forum, a main street, and a monumental gate inscribed to Hadrian.

vici:vici-62995.desc: Located within the Hellenistic-Roman city of Zeugma on the Euphrates, a Seleucid foundation that became a Roman legionary base and major commercial hub; the stadium is among the site's identified but still largely unexcavated public buildings.

vici:vici-63003.desc: Small odeon at Pompeiopolis, the Paphlagonian capital founded by Pompey the Great in 64 BCE (modern Taşköprü); German-led excavations resumed here in 2024 and exposed the structure beneath a roughly two-metre-thick ash layer.

vici:vici-63321.desc: Roman-era remains found in 1908 on Pisa's Via San Zeno during foundations work for the Institute of Physiology; a contemporary survey (kept at the Museo di San Matteo) never resolved whether the structure was a theatre or an amphitheatre, and it remains unexcavated, buried beneath the modern city.

vici:vici-63346.desc: Late 13th-century Byzantine palace built for co-emperor Constantine Palaiologos as an annex of the Blachernae complex; the best-preserved of Constantinople's three surviving Byzantine palaces, later used as an Ottoman menagerie, pottery workshop and Jewish poorhouse.

vici:vici-63435.desc: The 'Praetorium' at Umm el-Jimal, a Roman military headquarters built around AD 180 and named by excavator H. C. Butler in 1905; its cross-shaped domed hall was later remodeled into a luxurious Umayyad dwelling with mosaics and frescoes.

vici:vici-63461.desc: Roman theatre at the Emmatha hot springs (Hamat Gader), built in the 3rd century CE with about 2,000 basalt seats arranged in 15 rows around the orchestra, near baths begun by the Legio X Fretensis garrison from Gadara.

vici:vici-63475.desc: The southern end of Herod's hippodrome/stadium at Caesarea (built 10/9 BC, c.457x76m, seating ~38,000) was rebuilt in the 2nd century CE into an oval amphitheater for gladiatorial combat and venationes.

vici:vici-63557.desc: The Great Theatre of Roman Ilium (Troy), first built c.306 BC, was rebuilt under Augustus with a three-tiered scaenae frons (completed 12-11 BC); cavea diameter 98.6m, seating roughly 7,700-9,650.

vici:vici-63782.desc: A large Roman maritime villa near the Timavo river's mouth at Duino, popularly but wrongly linked to Attila; its terraced concrete substructure, over 35 metres long, overcomes a 10-metre drop toward the sea using techniques paralleling Campanian and Latial villas.

vici:vici-63803.desc: A medium-sized (c.70x100m) amphitheater of Forum Sempronii, identified in 2009 aerial photography near modern Fossombrone; its rubble-core walls faced in opus vittatum remain largely buried and unexcavated.

vici:vici-63970.desc: The Comitium, Rome's original open-air assembly and law-court space beside the Senate house; excavations by Giacomo Boni in 1899–1903 uncovered the Lapis Niger, an Old Latin inscription of c. 570–550 BC among the earliest known.

vici:vici-64584.desc: Despite the generic vici label "Circus," this point marks the Roman theatre of Alba Fucens, built 2nd-1st century BC on the Pettorino hill slope east of the Hercules sanctuary; no longer visible above ground today.

vici:vici-64631.desc: Greek Calatis (Heraclea Pontica colony, founded 6th c. BC; modern Mangalia, Romania) is attested by inscriptions as having had a theatre, but the building's physical remains have never been located archaeologically.

vici:vici-64633.desc: A late 2nd-century CE inscription honoring citizen Marcus Manlius Modestus Quietianus records his private funding of a theatre at Curubis (modern Korba, Tunisia); the building itself has never been located.

vici:vici-64635.desc: Theater of the Romano-Berber town Furnos Minus, identified with Henchir-el-Msaadine near Tebourba; a bishopric of Africa Proconsularis whose Christian cemetery yielded a celebrated 5th–6th-century mosaic of Daniel in the Lions' Den, now in Tunisia's Bardo Museum.

vici:vici-64636.desc: Theater of Gurza, a civitas of Roman Byzacena at modern Kalâa Kebira and later seat of an episcopal see (revived in 1933 as a Catholic titular bishopric); the theater is catalogued in Lachaux's survey of Proconsular African theaters.

vici:vici-64639.desc: Theater of Vallitanus (Sidi Medien), a Romano-Berber colonia and Africa Proconsularis bishopric under Carthage; the theater ruins there are documented in Frank Sear's architectural survey of Roman theaters, alongside inscriptions naming local officials.

vici:vici-64641.desc: Roman theater of Kissamos (Kastelli Kissamou), the free Roman city (civitas libera) that succeeded Hellenistic Kisamos as Polyrrhenia's port; first noted by 16th-century observer Onorio Belli, its seating and stage-building stones mapped by Thomas Spratt in the 19th century are now lost.

vici:vici-64642.desc: Theater of Antinoöpolis, the city Hadrian founded on 30 October AD 130 on the Nile in memory of his drowned companion Antinous; its ruins near the southern gate, still visible to Napoleonic surveyors and recorded in the Description de l'Égypte, were later quarried away for building stone.

vici:vici-64643.desc: Theater of Crocodilopolis/Arsinoe (Medinet el-Fayum), the Ptolemaic-Roman capital of the Faiyum and cult center of the crocodile god Sobek, whose sacred pool-kept crocodile Petsuchos, adorned with gold pendants, drew pilgrims and tourists into Roman times.

vici:vici-64645.desc: A vast 2nd-century AD Roman theatre excavated by Flinders Petrie, estimated to have seated up to 11,000 spectators — among the largest in Roman Egypt; later stripped as a stone quarry, leaving only fragmentary remains.

vici:vici-64647.desc: The chronicler John Malalas records that Vespasian built this theatre on the site of a former synagogue using spoils from the Jewish War; American and French teams excavated it in 1932-1940.

vici:vici-64648.desc: Roman-era theatre of Seleucia ad Calycadnum (modern Silifke); largely unexcavated, overgrown and built over, with its orchestra long used as a depot for local buses.

vici:vici-64650.desc: A theatre has long been conjectured for Hellenistic-Roman Kition (Larnaca) but has never been definitively located or excavated; most of the ancient city lies buried beneath the modern town.

vici:vici-64653.desc: This 2nd-century Roman amphitheatre of Narbo Martius measured about 122 x 93 m and could hold an estimated 25,000 spectators; its buried remains, located via 1838 surveys, underlie modern Narbonne's street plan.

vici:vici-64666.desc: Amphitheater of the Roman spa town Aquae Statiellae (modern Acqui Terme), built on the settlement's outskirts near the Via Aemilia Scauri and identified by excavation in the 1960s alongside the town's thermal baths.

vici:vici-65356.desc: Amphitheater of Roman Carpis (colonia Iulia Carpitana), a Cape Bon coastal town possibly founded under Caesar or Augustus; its rubble-masonry remains stand beside a large cistern that received an aqueduct at El Mraissa.

vici:vici-65731.desc: Forum of the Roman Lusitanian port city of Balsa, sited on the hill at Quinta da Torre d'Aires; despite Balsa's size, its urban core and forum remained undocumented until geophysical prospection and excavations finally located them in 2019.

vici:vici-65977.desc: Amphitheater of Roman Parma, known as the 'Arena' in ancient and medieval sources; sections of its perimeter wall found in 1846 and 1933 suggest a capacity near 25,000 seated spectators before its destruction in the 6th century.

vici:vici-66000.desc: Ancient Panormus is attested to have had a circus and amphitheater by a 2nd-century AD inscription honoring one Aurelianus for staging games; aerial-photo analysis has proposed a curving structure in the Loggia/Vucciria quarter, though the site remains debated.

vici:vici-66001.desc: Panormus's amphitheater is attested by a 2nd-century AD inscription from San Cataldo recording gladiatorial games and animal hunts sponsored by one Aurelianus; its exact location is debated, with this Loggia/Vucciria-quarter site suggested by aerial-photo curvature analysis.

vici:vici-66293.desc: Roman amphitheater of Augustodunum (Autun), built in the second half of the 1st century AD next to the city's famous theater; at roughly 154x130m it ranked among the largest in the Roman Empire, but was fully quarried away by the 18th century and rediscovered only through 1832 and 1844 excavations.

vici:vici-66294.desc: The Grohan amphitheater of Juliomagus (Angers), built around AD 115 with capacity for roughly 6,000 spectators; unusually well-documented from a 1637 survey by Claude Ménard, it survived until the 17th century before being destroyed, with the last traces lost to 19th-century street works.

vici:vici-66413.desc: Nevers occupies the site of Noviodunum, an Aeduan oppidum on the Loire where Caesar stockpiled Gallic hostages, grain, army pay and cavalry horses in 52 BC; the Aedui massacred the Roman garrison there and burned the town during the revolt recounted in his Gallic War, book VII. No specific theater structure at the site is confirmed by excavation.

vici:vici-66417.desc: Remains of a spectacle building at Aquae Sextiae (Aix-en-Provence), discovered only in 2004; the curvature of its excavated seating rows has not settled whether it was a semicircular theater or an oval amphitheater.

vici:vici-66446.desc: Roman theater of Tolosa (Toulouse), built under Augustus between the Place du Pont-Neuf and La Daurade with seating for about 8,000; its foundations were located in 1869 during construction of rue de Metz, but virtually nothing survives above ground today.

vici:vici-66499.desc: Square Castan in Besançon (Roman Vesontio): a semicircular hemicycle roughly 54m across, ringed by eight re-erected Corinthian columns, uncovered by Auguste Castan in 1870. Long identified as a Roman theater, specialists now favor reading it as a colonnaded podium of uncertain function tied to the Arcier aqueduct's water-distribution basin.

vici:vici-66536.desc: The Great Theatre of ancient Ambracia, discovered in 1981 near Arta's Tsakalov Street; the Corinthian colony became King Pyrrhus of Epirus's capital in 295 BC, who adorned it with theatres and palaces.

vici:vici-67575.desc: The stadium of Antioch in Pisidia (near Yalvaç), a Hellenistic-period structure restored in the 2nd century AD and later dismantled by Christians in late antiquity, served the Roman colony founded by Augustus in 25 BC.

vici:vici-67664.desc: The ruined governor's palace ('Kaisariye', imperial residence) of Saccaea, renamed Maximianopolis in AD 287; built in dark basalt around the mid-3rd century with a domed reception hall and niched corridors, remaining in use into the Byzantine period.

vici:vici-67760.desc: Lavish early 8th-century Umayyad desert palace and bath complex near Jericho, likely begun under Caliph Hisham; its bathhouse floor bears the 'Tree of Life' mosaic, one of the largest and finest figural mosaics surviving from the Islamic world.

vici:vici-67778.desc: Marketplace at Babisqa, a Byzantine-era village in Syria's 'Dead Cities'; a portico inscription dates the 33 m, five-chamber, two-storey arcaded structure to 547 CE, alongside two churches and the region's only certain public baths.

vici:vici-67867.desc: The Hellenistic odeon of Selinus in Cilicia, cut into a rectangular cavea seating roughly 1,000, stands near the spot where Emperor Trajan died on 8 August 117 AD, after which the town was briefly renamed Trajanopolis.

vici:vici-67883.desc: A stadium associated with the fortified Roman/Byzantine city of Lamos in Rough Cilicia, whose surviving gate bears a dedication to Emperor Gallienus; most standing remains date to the 2nd-3rd centuries AD.

vici:vici-68504.desc: The theatre of Mopsuestia (Misis) on the Pyramus (Ceyhan) river, a Cilician city legendarily founded by the seer Mopsus, whose surviving ruins also include a Roman bridge, stadium and colonnaded street.

vici:vici-68517.desc: The stadium of Apamea Kibotos (Celaenae) in Phrygia, founded by Antiochus I Soter and site of the 188 BC Treaty of Apamea between Rome and the Seleucids; partial remains survive near modern Dinar.

vici:vici-69282.desc: Seljuk summer palace complex of Sultan Kayqubad I (1220-1237) on the shore of Lake Beyşehir, its Great Palace measuring 50x35m; excavated from the 1960s, it yielded a renowned collection of polychrome figural ceramic tiles now in Konya's Karatay Museum.

vici:vici-69769.desc: Theatre of Ruessium, Gallo-Roman capital of the Vellavi civitas, apparently built around AD 50 under Claudius; its site was recently proposed via archival and cadastral research, comparable in scale to the theatres of Moingt and Javols.

vici:vici-69838.desc: Nero's Macellum Magnum, dedicated 59 CE on the Caelian Hill, was Rome's grand vaulted food market with marble counters and separate halls for butchers, fishmongers and produce sellers; its site likely underlies the church of S. Stefano Rotondo.

vici:vici-69855.desc: Theatre of Hypaepa, a Lydian city in the Cayster valley regarded as second only to Ephesus in regional importance; the theatre stood on the western slopes of the acropolis within the fortified city.

vici:vici-69910.desc: Site of the agora of Greek Neapolis, founded c. 470 BCE, later the Roman forum; it lay beneath today's Piazza San Gaetano, with a Roman-era macellum and shops excavated beneath the Basilica of San Lorenzo Maggiore since the 1990s.

vici:vici-69982.desc: Small Umayyad-period farmhouse-palace with an attached bathhouse and two olive presses, 1.75km northeast of Azraq castle; built of basalt directly on bedrock, with Roman and Byzantine pottery attesting earlier occupation of the site.

vici:vici-69983.desc: Umayyad desert palace built in 723 CE under Caliph Yazid II, 24 km southeast of Amman; little survives beyond carved acanthus capitals and a Kufic-inscribed water-depth gauge for its large cistern reservoir.

vici:vici-69984.desc: Among the oldest and most complete Umayyad desert castles, built c. 685-715 under Abd al-Malik or Yazid II; the complex's minaret is the only surviving Umayyad-era minaret, one of the oldest in the world.

vici:vici-69985.desc: Umayyad bathhouse built under Caliph Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik in the early 8th century, linked to nearby Qasr al-Hallabat 2 km west; its plan closely resembles the frescoed bath at Qusayr Amra.

vici:vici-69986.desc: Sprawling 8th-century Umayyad way-station on Wadi Mshash covering roughly 2 sq km, with a caravanserai-like building, bathhouse, reservoirs and cisterns serving traffic between Amman and Wadi Sirhan; excavated by the German Archaeological Institute since 2011.

vici:vici-69988.desc: Unfinished 8th-century Umayyad palace built c. 743 CE by order of Caliph al-Walid II for his sons, the southernmost of Jordan's desert castles, noted for its deep wells and early brick-vaulted construction.

vici:vici-69990.desc: Umayyad settlement (early 8th century) southeast of Madaba with two contemporary qasrs, a mosque, and remains from Bronze Age through Roman occupation; uniquely for the region, an Umayyad settlement built directly amid a Christian community.

vici:vici-70125.desc: Forum of Simitthus (Chemtou), Tunisia's source of prized golden Numidian marble; built over Numidian royal tombs and a native temple, only the basilica's massive apse survives. Excavated since the late 1960s by a Tunisian-German team led by Friedrich Rakob.

vici:vici-70239.desc: Remains of Corinth's Roman circus, identified as the 'Apsidal Building' uncovered in the 1967-68 Gymnasium-area excavations; planned into the Caesarian city's Augustan design, it hosted equestrian events of the Caesarea and Isthmian games.

vici:vici-70249.desc: Roman circus of Carmona, an estimated 290 m long and built into the local slope, part of the city's spectacle complex with its amphitheatre and necropolis; poorly documented and its construction date still uncertain.

vici:vici-70582.desc: Roman circus of Corduba (Cordoba), capital of Baetica, built between the reigns of Nero and Domitian at roughly 400 x 100 m with an ornamented spina; abandoned after structural failure by the mid-2nd century AD.

vici:vici-70674.desc: Open equestrian training ground in the Campus Martius, named for the triga (three-horse chariot); first attested under Claudius and used into the 4th century, kept free of permanent structures near the Tiber bend.

vici:vici-70699.desc: Theatre of Artaxata, the Hellenistic Artaxiad capital founded c. 176 BC on the Araxes; excavations have identified it as Armenia's earliest known theatre, evidence of the city's Greek-influenced civic and cultural life.

vici:vici-71498.desc: Site of ancient Forentum, a Daunian-Samnite settlement absorbed by Rome in 318/315 BC; excavations on the Gravetta hill have revealed a 3rd-century BC sanctuary and traces of an associated town.

vici:vici-71499.desc: Inscribed stone seating steps from the Roman theatre of Lopodunum (Ladenburg), found in the Burgacker district and identified in the 1960s by archaeologist Berndmark Heukemes; donor inscriptions name citizens such as Optatius Tetricius.

vici:vici-71652.desc: The Roman theatre of Ostra, excavated in 1903–04 and restored in 2020, measured only 44.6 m across with three tiers of seating — the smallest Roman theatre known in the Marche region.

vici:vici-71807.desc: Theatre remains within Potentia, a Roman colony founded 184 BC on the Adriatic coast to settle Punic War veterans; the town prospered on the terracotta trade before a 56 BC earthquake devastated it.

vici:vici-72087.desc: Fortified Middle Bronze Age city in the Jordan Valley with a monumental palace complex on the upper tell; excavated since 2005 by Trinity Southwest University, and proposed by some archaeologists as the site of biblical Sodom.

vici:vici-73102.desc: Palace and garden quarter of Nishapur founded by Tahirid governor Abdullah ibn Tahir al-Khurasani in the early 9th century; grew into an administrative and military settlement, later home to poet Attar of Nishapur, ruined by the 13th century.

vici:vici-73115.desc: The Gallo-Roman theatre of Neung-sur-Beuvron, dated late 1st–early 2nd century AD, formed part of a rural sanctuary with a temple and spring; c. 100 m across, it held perhaps 7,000 spectators and was used until the 3rd century.

vici:vici-73117.desc: A Gallo-Roman theatre discovered in 1834 near the probable site of Noviodunum of the Bituriges, but never excavated or documented since.

vici:vici-73275.desc: The Roman theatre of Antipolis is attested chiefly by the funerary stele of the child performer Septentrion, who ‘danced and was applauded’ on its stage; sited near the Porte Royale, it was demolished in 1691.

vici:vici-73276.desc: A Roman amphitheatre at Antipolis is known only from its oval outline on 17th–18th-century fortification plans of Antibes; its physical remains vanished during the town's refortification, and scholars still debate whether it truly existed.

vici:vici-73280.desc: An early Hellenistic theatre unearthed in 2016 by archaeologist Xeni Arapogianni on the Ellinika ridge at Thouria, near Kalamata; its 16.3 m limestone orchestra and first row of seats survive from when Thouria was the leading city of western Messenia.

vici:vici-73281.desc: A Roman-era theatre with orchestra, stone-backed seating and a niche was found in 2006 beside a Roman mausoleum and cemetery at Fiskardo (ancient Panormos), Kefalonia, during hotel construction.

vici:vici-73330.desc: Roman-period theatre at Savatra (Soatra), an autonomous Lycaonian city near modern Yağlıbayat, Turkey; excavations from 2021 exposed its stage, orchestra and cavea steps — a rare central-Anatolian example, said to have doubled as the city's council hall.

vici:vici-73352.desc: Earthen stadium in the sanctuary quarter of Dion, Macedonia, tied to the "Olympia" games traditionally founded under Archelaus I (late 5th c. BC); excavated in 1995, with coin finds spanning Alexander I to Philip V.

vici:vici-73353.desc: A ~32m stadium with three rows of stone seating, the only one known in western mainland Greece besides Olympia's, found in 2014 in the sanctuary of Athena and Poseidon on the acropolis of ancient Molykreion, Aetolia.

vici:vici-73358.desc: U-shaped horse- and chariot-racing course on Delos (west side ~300m long), edged by a terrace wall built for Athens' 5th-century BC revival of the games, with a horse-watering well near the presumed starting line.

vici:vici-73370.desc: Stadium of Megalopolis, capital of the Arcadian League founded 371–368 BC; Pausanias records a temple of Dionysus on its east side and a spring sacred to the god within it, adjoining the city's colossal theatre.

vici:vici-73795.desc: Multi-period site on the River Wharfe with two successive Roman forts, temporary camps and a vicus guarding the Lincoln–Aldborough road crossing; no securely attested Roman theatre survives, though the adjacent Neolithic henge has been speculated to have been reused as an amphitheatre.

vici:vici-74491.desc: Seleucid city (earlier Pythopolis), founded/enlarged by Antiochus I at the Maeander–Orsinus confluence in Caria; ancient sources attest both a theatre and a stadium, of which a stone Roman-period stadium ~200m long survives near modern Kuyucak.

vici:vici-76437.desc: Forum of Roman Tolosa (Toulouse), excavated in 1992-93 beneath Place Esquirol; its 27 x 35 m podium temple, probably the Capitolium of Jupiter, Juno and Minerva, was built in the later 1st century CE and destroyed around 400 CE.

vici:vici-77277.desc: Pavement of the forum of Roman Mediolanum (Milan), a 55 x 160 m plaza built under Augustus at the crossing of the cardo and decumanus; paving slabs and drainage remains were found beneath the Ambrosiana library and the crypt of San Sepolcro.

vici:vici-78057.desc: Hellenistic-era theatre of ancient Syros; its ashlar seating blocks survive incorporated into later buildings near Ano Syra, one of the few traces of the island's ancient public architecture.

vici:vici-78059.desc: Theatre of Pagai (Pagae), a fortified Megarian harbour city on the Corinthian Gulf near modern Kato Alepochori, strategically important from the Persian Wars through the Peloponnesian War as recorded by Thucydides.

vici:vici-7892.desc: Small Roman odeon seating about 500, built in the 2nd century CE (likely under Antoninus Pius or Marcus Aurelius) beside the larger Roman Theatre of Philadelphia; used for music, poetry, and lectures.

vici:vici-78993.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre uncovered in a 2022 rescue excavation near Metz, on the Roman road to Reims via Verdun; estimated to have held up to 6,000 spectators, possibly at the site of ancient Ibliodurum.

vici:vici-7905.desc: Roman theatre built under Augustus as part of Glanum's monumental rebuilding alongside temples, a basilica, baths, and a triumphal arch, on a Hellenistic town site centered on a sacred healing spring.

vici:vici-7910.desc: A U-shaped underground horreum built c. 100 AD to support the forum of Roman Durocortorum (Reims); rediscovered in 1838, only its eastern gallery survives, one of just four such cryptoporticus galleries known in France.

vici:vici-80853.desc: Chariot-racing circus at Hadrumetum (Sousse); its existence is attested chiefly by 3rd-century AD mosaics, including one from the House of Sorothus naming winning circus horses such as Adorandus and Crinitus.

vici:vici-80875.desc: Roman hippodrome of Caesarea Mazaca (Kayseri), capital of Roman Cappadocia from Tiberius's reign; buried beneath the modern city, its roughly 450 m track was located in 2024 via old maps and geophysical survey.

vici:vici-81143.desc: The imperial hippodrome of Sirmium, built c. 312–324 under Licinius or Constantine beside the tetrarchic imperial palace; roughly 450 x 150 m and seating up to 10,000, it is the only known unexcavated Roman hippodrome, lying buried beneath modern Sremska Mitrovica.

vici:vici-81435.desc: One of three fortified palaces at Lashkari Bazar, the Ghaznavid rulers' winter residence near Bost; the Central Palace (32x52m) may predate the adjoining Ghaznavid North and South Palaces, possibly dating to the Samanid period.

vici:vici-8145.desc: One of a rare pair of earthen amphitheatres in the vicus of the Flavian-era auxiliary fort Zugmantel on the Upper Germanic Limes; an oval roughly 50 m across ringed by a 13 m-wide, 1.5 m-high earth bank, likely combining spectacle with troop training ground.

vici:vici-8146.desc: The second of two earthen amphitheatres flanking the civilian settlement of the Roman fort Zugmantel on the Upper Germanic Limes — amphitheatres are very rare on the frontier, and this pair likely served both as performance venues and as training grounds for the garrison.

vici:vici-81558.desc: Theatre of Roman Augusta in Cilicia, a colony founded in 20 AD and named for Livia; a 1955 rescue survey recorded its open-air auditorium (c. 62 m across) along with a triumphal arch, colonnaded streets, and baths just before the site was submerged by the new Seyhan Dam reservoir.

vici:vici-81602.desc: Bronze Age palace atop Tepe A at Mundigak, excavated by Jean-Marie Casal for DAFA (1951-58); its Period IV-V facade of engaged semi-columns marked the settlement's growth into a fortified city with palace and temple.

vici:vici-82733.desc: Site of Troesmis, a Roman legionary fortress on the Lower Danube limes, home of Legio V Macedonica (107–163) and later a municipium under Marcus Aurelius (c. 177); no amphitheatre here is independently confirmed in the literature.

vici:vici-82904.desc: Aquae Flaviae, a thermal spa town in Roman Gallaecia named for the Flavian dynasty, raised to municipium status in 79 CE under Vespasian; famed for its hot springs and later spanned by Trajan's bridge over the Tâmega.

vici:vici-83086.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre-amphitheatre hybrid in Lutetia (Paris), built in the 1st century CE and seating up to 15,000; sacked in the 275 raids, later filled in, then rediscovered by Théodore Vacquer in the 1860s and saved after Victor Hugo's campaign.

vici:vici-83134.desc: Hradisko hilltop fortress near Mušov, the principal Roman military base of the Marcomannic Wars (172–180), built deep in Germanic territory under Marcus Aurelius; excavations found a bath complex, but no theatre or amphitheatre is attested at the site.

vici:vici-83640.desc: A circular paved forum about 40 m across, built under Emperor Anastasius I in the late 6th century at Dyrrhachium (Durrës), echoing Constantinople's Forum of Constantine; long misidentified as a macellum before excavation clarified its civic function.

vici:vici-84196.desc: Cadyanda's Hellenistic theatre, built late 2nd–early 1st century BCE, was repaired and reused under Roman rule; about 20 rows of seating survive on the hillside above Üzümlü, though the stage building has been destroyed.

vici:vici-84198.desc: Theater identified in 1971 in Vibo Valentia's Terravecchia quarter (ancient Hipponion, a Greek colony of Locri Epizefiri, refounded as the Latin colony Valentia in 192 BC); only retaining walls and part of the lower cavea's steps survive.

vici:vici-84203.desc: Part of the Sanctuary of Diana Nemorensis at Lake Nemi (nemus Aricinum), founded c. 300 BC around the cult of Diana of the Wood; the complex also had baths, a nymphaeum and pilgrim lodgings for the annual August Nemoralia festival.

vici:vici-84206.desc: Theater of the Roman colony Minervia Scolacium, founded 123-122 BC by Gaius Gracchus on the Greek site of Skylletion; built into the hillside with room for c. 3,500 spectators, it flourished under Nerva's refoundation around AD 96.

vici:vici-84213.desc: An AD 148 inscription from the hilltop town of Mons Feretrius (modern San Leo) records a marble-adorned theatre — "theatrum marmoribus ornatum" — the principal evidence for the building, whose remains are not extensively excavated.

vici:vici-84214.desc: The Roman theatre of Pisaurum (Pesaro) lies beneath the former Filanda silk-mill on Via De Amicis; excavations resumed there in 2021 under the Marche Superintendency as part of plans for a future museum of Roman Pesaro.

vici:vici-84215.desc: The theatre of Pitinum Mergens, identified near Catirano along the Via Flaminia by University of Urbino excavations, had a stage set parallel to the road and two concentric corridors supporting a cavea about 70 m in diameter.

vici:vici-84216.desc: Built in the Augustan era on the slope of Genoa's San Giusto hill overlooking the harbor and restored under Trajan by Q. Petronius Modestus, the theatre stayed in use into late antiquity (even hosting gladiatorial shows); identified in 1814, excavated in 1938.

vici:vici-84217.desc: Theatre of Roman Pollentia (Pollenzo), built in the earlier 1st century AD with a two-tier cavea about 74 m across and a 34 m orchestra, seating roughly 6,000 — the largest Roman theatre known in Piedmont.

vici:vici-84220.desc: Roman theatre of Laus Pompeia (Lodi Vecchio): so little structural fabric survives along the presumed main street that scholars could attempt only a virtual reconstruction of its stage building.

vici:vici-84222.desc: Per Diodorus Siculus, a native of Agyrium, its theatre — part of Timoleon's building program after 339 BC, when he settled 10,000 Greek colonists there — ranked as the finest in Sicily after Syracuse's; little survives beneath modern Agira.

vici:vici-84223.desc: No physical remains of a theatre have ever been found at Gela; its existence is known only from literary tradition. The tragedian Aeschylus, active across Sicily, died in Gela in 456 BC.

vici:vici-84224.desc: In 214 BC, during the Second Punic War, the Roman garrison under Lucius Pinarius massacred Henna's citizens after gathering them in the town's theatre on suspicion of planning to defect to Carthage (Livy 24.37–39).

vici:vici-84225.desc: Part of Cassinomagus's monumental Gallo-Roman sanctuary at Chassenon, this horseshoe-shaped spectacle building ("la Léna"), roughly 70–80m across, stood alongside the site's famed 2nd-century thermal baths and temple complex.

vici:vici-84227.desc: Built in successive phases from the 1st to 3rd century AD, the Gallo-Roman theatre of Derventum (Drevant) measured 85m in diameter and held an estimated 5,300 spectators, remaining in use into the 4th century.

vici:vici-84233.desc: Excavations in 1969–73 at the Treveran vicus Belginum (Wederath) uncovered a small Gallo-Roman cult theatre within its temple precinct, part of a settlement occupied continuously from about 600 BC to 400 AD.

vici:vici-84234.desc: At the vicus Contiomagus (Pachten, Dillingen/Saar), inscribed sandstone seating blocks excavated in 1961–63 are interpreted as remains of a small 2nd-century cult theatre tied to a temple complex — the only confirmed theatre site in the Saarland.

vici:vici-84236.desc: Built in the later 1st century AD at Verbinum, a Gallo-Roman vicus at the crossroads of roads linking Bavay–Reims and Saint-Quentin–Macquenoise, this theatre held several thousand spectators; excavated since 1870, its stage wall bears traces of destruction by fire.

vici:vici-84239.desc: The theater of Forum Segusiavorum (Feurs), chief town of the Segusiavi, is known only from an inscription; as in other small Gallic towns it likely had wooden rather than stone tiered seating.

vici:vici-84253.desc: Built in the 1st century AD on the hillside above Lillebonne and enlarged in the 2nd-3rd centuries, this hybrid theater-amphitheater measured about 106 x 94 m and seated over 5,000; identified in 1764 by the Comte de Caylus.

vici:vici-84254.desc: Likely the hybrid theater-amphitheater known as 'de Grohan,' built around 115 CE with seating for roughly 6,000; unusually for a Gallo-Roman arena it is documented in records as early as 1529, before its 17th-century destruction.

vici:vici-84255.desc: Built into a hillside during the reign of Claudius (41-54 CE), with a 75 m facade and a 24 m cavea radius, this theater at Evreux was excavated in 1843 then razed by the property owner.

vici:vici-84268.desc: A Roman theater stood at Iader (Zadar) into the early modern era before being demolished in 1657 to make way for a Venetian fortress built on the site.

vici:vici-84271.desc: A theatre is attested for Lucurgentum (Roman-era Morón de la Frontera, near Hispalis in Baetica), inferred from ancient literary/epigraphic testimony catalogued in Sear's 'Roman Theatres'; no physical remains have ever been identified or excavated.

vici:vici-84272.desc: A theatre is recorded for Tucci (Colonia Augusta Gemella Tuccitana, modern Martos, Jaén) purely from literary and epigraphic references to spectacles; no physical remains have been found, and Sear's catalogue lists it among Hispania's inferred, unexcavated theatres.

vici:vici-84273.desc: A theatre is listed for Roman Mago (modern Mahón, Menorca) in Sear's catalogue of Roman theatres, broadly dated to the 1st century; no remains have been located, and its existence rests on inference rather than excavation.

vici:vici-84274.desc: The theatre of Panopolis (Akhmim), a city associated with the cult of Pan, is attested in Roman-period papyri as a venue for festivals and athletic games, but its physical remains have never been substantially excavated or located.

vici:vici-84275.desc: A small semicircular Roman odeon-theatre was excavated at Antipatris (Tel Afek), a city founded by Herod the Great and named for his father Antipater; it stood at the end of the site's colonnaded cardo and hosted musical performances.

vici:vici-84276.desc: The only known theatre of the central Negev, excavated in part by Avraham Negev at the Nabataean-Roman-Byzantine city of Elusa (Haluza); built on a roughly 27 m axis with a maximum diameter of about 34.5 m, oriented southeast-northwest.

vici:vici-84277.desc: At Arethusa (modern Al-Rastan on the Orontes), a Seleucid foundation traditionally credited to Seleucus I Nicator, the French archaeologist Edmond Frézouls recorded surviving cavea seating rows of a Roman theatre in his 1952 survey of Syria's theatres.

vici:vici-84278.desc: At Shaqqa, renamed Maximianopolis in AD 287 for co-emperor Maximian and raised to colonia and probable garrison-town status, a Roman theatre built of local black basalt survives among the town's well-preserved kalybe, 'Kaisariye', and temple of Zeus Megistos.

vici:vici-84294.desc: Theatre of Ceramus (Keramos), a Carian city on the Ceramic Gulf near modern Ören; Hellenized by the 6th century BC, it joined the Athenian League and later the Chrysaoric League, minting its own coinage in Roman times.

vici:vici-84300.desc: Ancient theatre on the acropolis hill of Kolonna, Aegina, described by Pausanias (2nd century AD) as comparable in size and workmanship to the theatre of Epidaurus; the hill has been excavated since Charles Robert Cockerell's 1811 dig.

vici:vici-84301.desc: Ancient stadium adjoining the theatre on the Kolonna acropolis at Aegina, part of the sanctuary complex first explored in Cockerell's 1811 excavation and further investigated in 1903, 1924 and 1966.

vici:vici-84416.desc: Achaemenid-period palace (site BH48) near Borazjan, excavated by Esmail Yaghmaei from the 1970s; the structure has since been entirely bulldozed and survives only in excavation records and publications.

vici:vici-84419.desc: Achaemenid palace near Borazjan with a colonnaded hall comparable in plan to Pasargadae; unearthed in 1977 by Esmail Yaghmaei, with a 2005 dig yielding a relief of Darius I and gold sheets buried beside a column base.

vici:vici-8497.desc: Flavian-era macellum (market hall) of Roman Puteoli, excavated 1750-53; discovery of a Serapis statue there caused it to be misidentified as a Temple of Serapis until Charles Dubois recognized its commercial function in 1907.

vici:vici-8508.desc: Roman theatre at Scolacium (Colonia Minervia Scolacium, built over the Greek city of Skylletion), raised on a hillside in the 1st century AD with seating for roughly 5,000; it stands within the archaeological park near Roccelletta di Borgia, Calabria.

vici:vici-8510.desc: Excavated remains of the forum of Roman Arausio (Orange), founded 35 BC for veterans of Legio II Augusta; 1920s digs revealed a basilica, temple, and curia beneath the modern town center.

vici:vici-8516.desc: Odeon of Roman Catania, probably built in the 2nd century AD beside the main theatre on ground once linked to a sanctuary of Demeter and Kore; it seated about 1,500 for musical and poetic contests and rehearsals, and was fully excavated in the 19th century.

vici:vici-86800.desc: Forum and urban baths of Sentinum, the Umbrian town near which Rome's legions under Fabius Rullianus and Decius Mus crushed a Samnite-Gallic-Etruscan-Umbrian coalition at the decisive Battle of Sentinum in 295 BC.

vici:vici-87102.desc: Theatre of Kitanaura, a Lycian hill city near Saraycık in Antalya's Kumluca district, unmentioned by any ancient author; its identity was fixed only through a 1998 coin study and a Roman milestone found at Patara in 1993, with fortifications spanning Hellenistic to Byzantine phases.

vici:vici-87375.desc: A theatre within a sanctuary north of ancient Loryma on the Bozburun Peninsula, likely predating the Hellenistic period. Its wall construction matches 4th-century-BCE work at nearby Amos, and its plan exceeds a semicircle; no stage building has been found.

vici:vici-89755.desc: Ghaznavid royal palace at Ghazni built for Sultan Mas'ud III (r. 1099-1115); excavated 1957-66, its courtyard bore a marble dado of roughly 510 carved panels with Persian verses praising Mahmud of Ghazna.

vici:vici-9852.desc: Roman theatre of Capitolias (Beit Ras), a Decapolis city founded c. 97-98 CE under Nerva or Trajan and named for Jupiter Capitolinus. Archaeoseismic study identified at least two ancient earthquake phases that damaged and helped end its use.

vici:vici-9993.desc: Forum of Roman Karales (Cagliari), sited beneath today's Piazza del Carmine; surviving inscriptions attest surrounding temples, granaries, the praetorium, a courthouse, and at least three bath complexes.
